# MODULE 1: SOIL EROSION - FUNDAMENTALS, MECHANICS & QUANTIFICATION

## 1.1 Introduction to Soil Erosion

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Standard):**
> Soil erosion is the detachment, transportation, and deposition of soil particles from one location to another by the action of natural geological agents (water, wind, ice, and gravity) and accelerated anthropogenic activities. It is a three-phase geomorphic process involving **detachment → transport → deposition**.

**Pedological vs. Geological Erosion:**
- **Pedological (Normal/Geological) Erosion:** Occurs at a rate equal to or less than the rate of soil formation (≈ **0.025 to 0.25 mm/year** or **1 inch per 500–1000 years**). It is a natural, slow process that maintains the soil's mature profile.
- **Accelerated Erosion:** Occurs at a rate significantly greater than soil formation, primarily induced by human activities such as deforestation, overgrazing, and improper agricultural practices.

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Analogy (The Candle Wax Intuition):**
> Imagine a burning candle. As the wax melts (analogous to soil formation from bedrock weathering), the molten wax drips down (geological erosion - slow, natural). Now imagine blowing strongly on the candle (deforestation / ploughing) - the wax flies off rapidly. This **rapid wax loss** is accelerated erosion. The candle (Earth's soil) cannot regenerate wax (soil) fast enough to keep up.

### 1.2 Agents of Soil Erosion

| Agent | Primary Mechanism | Typical Severity |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Water** | Raindrop impact (splash), sheet flow, channelized runoff | **Most dominant globally** (~ 56% of total erosion) |
| **Wind** | Aeolian detachment of dry, loose particles | Severe in arid & semi-arid regions |
| **Gravity** | Mass wasting - landslides, slumps, creep | Steep mountainous terrain |
| **Ice / Glaciers** | Plucking, abrasion, freeze-thaw cycles | Polar / high-altitude regions |
| **Temperature** | Thermal expansion/contraction, freeze-thaw weathering | Tundra, periglacial zones |

### 1.3 Causes of Soil Erosion

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Classification of Causes:**

**A. Natural Causes (Geological):**
1. Heavy and intense rainfall (high kinetic energy)
2. Steep topography (high slope gradient)
3. Sparse vegetative cover
4. Soil texture (sandy > silty > clay in detachability)
5. Wind velocity and direction
6. Geological structure (faults, joints, weak rocks)

**B. Anthropogenic (Man-Made) Causes:**
1. **Deforestation** - removal of root binding network
2. **Overgrazing** - destruction of grass cover
3. **Improper agricultural practices** - up-down slope ploughing
4. **Mining and quarrying** - surface disturbance
5. **Urbanization and construction** - impervious surfaces increase runoff
6. **Fires** - destroy organic litter layer

### 1.4 Factors Affecting Soil Erosion

The four universal factors governing water erosion magnitude are codified in the **USLE (Universal Soil Loss Equation)** and follow the mnemonic **C-R-K-L-S-P** (Climate, Relief, Soil, Land cover, Slope, Practice).

$$A = f(Climate, \ Soil, \ Topography, \ Vegetation, \ Human \ Activity)$$

**Detailed Factor Analysis:**

1. **Climatic Factors**
   - Rainfall intensity (I) - measured in mm/hr
   - Rainfall duration
   - Rainfall kinetic energy
   - Wind velocity
   - Temperature (affects vegetation & freeze-thaw)

2. **Soil Characteristics**
   - Texture (sandy loams are most erodible)
   - Structure (granular > blocky > platy in resistance)
   - Organic matter content
   - Infiltration capacity
   - Moisture content
   - **Soil erodibility factor (K)** in USLE

3. **Topographic Factors**
   - Slope gradient (S) - percentage or ratio
   - Slope length (L)
   - Slope shape (uniform, convex, concave)
   - Aspect (direction facing)

4. **Vegetative Cover (C-factor)**
   - Type of vegetation (forest > grass > crop)
   - Density and canopy cover
   - Root depth and density
   - Growth stage

5. **Conservation Practices (P-factor)**
   - Contour bunding, terracing
   - Strip cropping
   - Mulching
   - Conservation tillage

### 1.5 Effects of Soil Erosion

> [!WARNING]
> **On-site (Direct) Effects:**
> - Loss of fertile topsoil (containing N, P, K, organic matter)
> - Reduced soil depth → poor root anchorage
> - Decreased water-holding capacity
> - Land degradation → reduced agricultural productivity
> - Gully formation → land fragmentation (may convert arable land to wasteland)
> - Silting of reservoirs and tanks → reduced storage capacity

> [!WARNING]
> **Off-site (Indirect) Effects:**
> - **Reservoir sedimentation** - major KTU topic (covered in §1.12)
> - Flooding due to reduced infiltration
> - Water pollution (sediment-bound nutrients, pesticides)
> - Damage to downstream infrastructure
> - Loss of aquatic habitats
> - Increased treatment cost for municipal water

# DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 Mechanics of Water Erosion

Water erosion is a **three-phase sequential process**:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Phase 1: Detachment** - Soil particles are separated from the soil mass by the eroding agent (raindrop impact or flowing water's shear stress exceeding soil's critical shear resistance).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Phase 2: Transportation** - Detached particles are carried by the agent. The transport capacity depends on the **kinetic energy** of the agent and the particle size.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Phase 3: Deposition** - When the agent's energy falls below the settling velocity threshold, particles are deposited. Coarse particles deposit first (in upper catchment), fine particles travel further (silt in tanks, clay to oceans).

## 2.2 Forms of Water Erosion

### 2.2.1 Splash Erosion (Raindrop Erosion)
- **Mechanism:** Kinetic energy of falling raindrops directly detaches soil particles. The raindrop's impact creates a small crater and splashes soil particles up to **0.6 - 0.9 m horizontally** and **0.5 m vertically**.
- **Threshold rainfall intensity:** > **25 mm/hr** is considered erosive (basis of KE>25 index).
- **Drop diameter:** Typically **0.5 - 6 mm**; terminal velocity ≈ **9 m/s** for 3 mm drops.

### 2.2.2 Sheet Erosion
- **Mechanism:** Uniform removal of a thin layer of soil across a broad, sloping surface by overland flow (sheet flow) that is **not yet channelized**.
- **Depth of soil removed per event:** Often 1–2 mm, undetectable visually but cumulatively devastating.
- **Visibility:** Most insidious form - rarely noticed by farmers.

### 2.2.3 Rill Erosion
- **Mechanism:** Concentrated overland flow forms small, parallel, shallow channels (rills) that are typically **< 0.3 m deep** and can be obliterated by tillage.
- **Critical shear stress:** When flow shear > soil's critical shear, detachment begins.

### 2.2.4 Gully Erosion
- **Mechanism:** Advanced rills coalesce into deep, permanent channels that **cannot be removed by ordinary tillage** and require special conservation measures.
- **Cross-section:** V-shaped or U-shaped; depth typically **> 0.3 - 0.5 m**.

### 2.2.5 Ravine Erosion
- **Mechanism:** Gullies that have progressed to a stage where they form a network of deep channels with steep, often vertical, sidewalls; **gully depth > 3 m** in Indian classification.
- **Land fragmentation:** Cultivation becomes impossible.

### 2.2.6 Stream Bank Erosion
- **Mechanism:** Lateral erosion of stream/river banks by flowing water, especially during high-velocity flood events. Often involves **meandering migration** and **cut-bank erosion**.

## 2.3 Classification of Gullies

Gullies are classified based on **shape, depth, and cross-section** as per the **Bureau of Indian Standards (BIS) and ICAR** classification:

| Class | Depth | Cross-Section | Remark |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Class A (Small)** | < 0.5 m | V-shape | Cultivable with modifications |
| **Class B (Medium)** | 0.5 - 1.5 m | U-shape | Marginal cultivation |
| **Class C (Deep)** | 1.5 - 3.0 m | U-shape | Non-cultivable |
| **Class D (Very Deep/Ravine)** | > 3.0 m | Steep walls | Ravine land; rehabilitation difficult |

## 2.4 Stages of Gully Development (Five Stages)

Based on the work of **Ireland, Schumm & Zimpfer (1939)** - the **Five Classical Stages**:

> [!NOTE]
> **Stage 1: Sheet Erosion / Initial Channel Formation**
> Overland flow concentrates in natural depressions. Headcut begins to form.

> [!NOTE]
> **Stage 2: Development of Erosion Channel**
> Active headcut migration upstream. Channel widens and deepens. V-shaped cross-section.

> [!NOTE]
> **Stage 3: Headcut Stabilization & Widening**
> Headcut reaches a stable slope (≈ **0.5 - 2%**). Channel widens by sidewall collapse. U-shape begins.

> [!NOTE]
> **Stage 4: Healing / Vegetation Establishment**
> Slope stabilizes. Vegetation colonizes sidewalls. Gully depth decreases.

> [!NOTE]
> **Stage 5: Equilibrium / Rejuvenation Phase**
> Gully reaches a graded profile. Further erosion only under extreme events.

## 2.5 Universal Soil Loss Equation (USLE) - Wischmeier & Smith (1965)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The foundational KTU equation** for predicting average annual soil loss from sheet and rill erosion:

$$A = R \times K \times L \times S \times C \times P$$

**Where:**
- $A$ = Estimated average annual soil loss (tonnes/hectare/year)
- $R$ = **Rainfall erosivity factor** (MJ·mm/ha·hr·yr)
- $K$ = **Soil erodibility factor** (tonnes·hr/MJ·mm)
- $L$ = **Slope length factor** (dimensionless)
- $S$ = **Slope steepness factor** (dimensionless)
- $C$ = **Cover and management factor** (dimensionless, 0–1)
- $P$ = **Conservation practice factor** (dimensionless, 0–1)

### 2.5.1 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| Factor | Symbol | Range | Unit | Represents |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Soil Loss | $A$ | — | t/ha/yr | Predicted average annual soil loss |
| Rainfall Erosivity | $R$ | 100 - 1000 | MJ·mm/(ha·hr·yr) | Erosive power of rainfall |
| Soil Erodibility | $K$ | 0.02 - 0.75 | t·h/(MJ·mm) | Susceptibility of soil to erosion |
| Slope Length | $L$ | 0 - 10+ | dimensionless | Effect of slope length |
| Slope Steepness | $S$ | 0 - 10+ | dimensionless | Effect of slope gradient |
| Cover Management | $C$ | 0 - 1.5 | dimensionless | Effect of vegetation/management |
| Conservation Practice | $P$ | 0.1 - 1.0 | dimensionless | Effect of conservation practices |

## 2.6 Modified USLE (MUSLE) - Williams (1975)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **Modified Universal Soil Loss Equation (MUSLE)** replaced the **R** factor with a **runoff factor** to estimate **single-event sediment yield** from a storm:

$$Y = 11.8 \times (Q \times q_p)^{0.56} \times K \times LS \times C \times P$$

**Where:**
- $Y$ = Sediment yield from a single storm event (**tonnes**)
- $Q$ = Storm runoff volume (**m³**)
- $q_p$ = Peak runoff rate (**m³/s**)
- $11.8$ = Empirical coefficient
- All other factors ($K, L, S, C, P$) are same as USLE

**Key Difference from USLE:** MUSLE predicts **single-event sediment yield** (not long-term average) and uses **runoff parameters** instead of rainfall erosivity. The exponent **0.56** on $(Q \cdot q_p)$ is empirically derived.

## 2.7 Rainfall Erosivity (R-Factor) - KE>25 and EI30 Methods

### 2.7.1 KE>25 Method (Kinetic Energy Method)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Only storms with intensity > 25 mm/hr** are considered erosive. The total kinetic energy of such storms is summed annually:

$$R_{KE>25} = \sum_{i=1}^{N} (KE)_i$$

**Kinetic energy per mm of rainfall (in MJ/ha):**

$$e = 0.119 + 0.0873 \log_{10} I$$

where $I$ = rainfall intensity (mm/hr).

**Total kinetic energy of a storm:**

$$KE = \sum_{j} (e_j \times \Delta V_j)$$

where $\Delta V_j$ = rainfall volume (depth in mm) in time step $j$.

**Units of R (KE>25):** **J/m²** or **MJ/ha**.

### 2.7.2 EI30 Method (Wischmeier & Smith, 1978) - Most Accepted

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **EI30** is the **product of total storm kinetic energy (E)** and the **maximum 30-minute rainfall intensity (I30)**. It is the **internationally accepted R-factor** for USLE.

$$R = E \times I_{30}$$

**Where:**
- $E$ = Total kinetic energy of the storm (MJ/ha)
- $I_{30}$ = Maximum 30-min rainfall intensity (mm/hr)
- $R$ units: **MJ·mm/(ha·hr·yr)** when summed annually

**Calculation of E:**

$$E = \sum_{r=1}^{m} e_r \cdot \Delta V_r$$

where $e_r$ is unit kinetic energy of rainfall segment $r$ and $\Delta V_r$ is rainfall depth in that segment.

**Empirical kinetic energy equation (Foster et al.):**

$$e = 0.29 \times [1 - 0.72 \exp(-0.05 \cdot I)]$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Note on KE>25 vs EI30:**
> KE>25 considers only storms exceeding 25 mm/hr. EI30 includes **all erosive storms** and weights by peak intensity, making it more accurate for both humid and arid regions. **KTU exams usually ask EI30.**

## 2.8 Soil Erodibility (K-Factor) - Detailed

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **K-factor** quantifies a soil's intrinsic susceptibility to erosion by rainfall and runoff, **per unit of rainfall erosivity**. It is determined by:
> - Soil texture (% sand, silt, clay, fine sand)
> - Organic matter content
> - Soil structure and permeability

**Wischmeier & Smith Nomograph Equation (approximate):**

$$K = 2.1 \times 10^{-4} \cdot M^{1.14} \cdot (12 - OM) + 3.25 \cdot (s-2) + 2.5 \cdot (p-3) \times 10^{-2}$$

where $M$ = (silt% + very fine sand%) × (100 - clay%); $OM$ = organic matter %; $s$ = structure code; $p$ = permeability code.

**Typical K-values for Indian soils:** **0.05 - 0.55 t·h/(MJ·mm)**. **Sandy loams** are most erodible (K ≈ 0.25 - 0.40).

## 2.9 Topographic Factor (LS)

$$LS = \left(\frac{\lambda}{22.13}\right)^m \times \left(0.065 + 0.045 s + 0.0065 s^2\right)$$

where $\lambda$ = slope length (m), $s$ = slope gradient (%), $m$ = slope-length exponent (0.2 for slopes < 1%, 0.3 for 1–3%, 0.4 for 3–5%, 0.5 for > 5%).

## 2.10 Crop Management Factor (C)

The **C-factor** is the ratio of soil loss from land under specified cover to that from clean-tilled, continuous fallow. C = 0 for permanent forest; C = 1 for bare soil. **It is the most effective factor for erosion control.**

## 2.11 Conservation Practice Factor (P)

The **P-factor** accounts for erosion-control practices:
- Up-and-down slope ploughing: P = 1.0
- Contour ploughing: P = 0.5 - 0.6
- Strip cropping: P = 0.3 - 0.4
- Terracing: P = 0.1 - 0.3

## 2.12 Measurement of Soil Erosion

### 2.12.1 Runoff Plots
- **Definition:** Bounded rectangular field plots (typically **22.13 m long × 1.83 m wide** for standard USLE plots) used to directly measure runoff and soil loss.
- **Components:** Metal or concrete borders, runoff collection trough, storage tanks (often multi-tank divisor system), sediment measurement.

### 2.12.2 Soil Samplers (Erosion Pins, Sediment Samplers)
- **Erosion Pins:** Metal or wooden pins driven into the soil surface; their exposed length changes with erosion or deposition, measured periodically.
- **Suspended Sediment Samplers:** USGS-type depth-integrating samplers (e.g., **DH-48, DH-59, US D-74**) collect water-sediment mixture at various depths.
- **Bed Load Samplers:** **Helley-Smith sampler** (US) or **Pit trap** for bed load.

## 2.13 Land Capability Classification (LCC)

Developed by the **USDA Soil Conservation Service (now NRCS)** - classifies land into **8 classes** based on limitations for agricultural use:

| Class | Description | Use |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Class I** | Few limitations, suited to cultivation | Wide range of crops |
| **Class II** | Moderate limitations | Cultivation with simple practices |
| **Class III** | Severe limitations | Cultivation with intensive practices |
| **Class IV** | Very severe limitations | Limited cultivation; pasture/forest |
| **Class V** | Few erosion risk but wet/stony; **not arable** | Pasture, woodland, wildlife |
| **Class VI** | Severe limitations; **not arable** | Pasture, woodland, watershed |
| **Class VII** | Very severe limitations; **not arable** | Restricted pasture, woodland, recreation |
| **Class VIII** | Limitations precluding use for plants | Wildlife, water supply, recreation |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Sub-classes (limitations):**
> - **e** = erosion risk
> - **w** = wetness (drainage)
> - **s** = shallowness (root zone)
> - **c** = climatic limitation
> Example: **IIIe** = Class III land with severe erosion limitation.

## 2.14 Rate of Sedimentation, Silt Monitoring & Storage Loss in Tanks

### 2.14.1 Sediment Yield
- **Definition:** Total sediment reaching a point of interest (e.g., a reservoir) per unit time.
- **Units:** **tonnes/yr** or **m³/yr** (using bulk density of sediment ≈ **1.4 - 1.6 t/m³**).

### 2.14.2 Silt Monitoring Methods
1. **Reservoir Survey Method** (Hydrographic survey) - most accurate for storage loss.
2. **Inflow-Outflow Sediment Sampling** - using suspended sediment samplers.
3. **Trap Efficiency Method** (Brune's or Churchill's curve) - estimates sediment deposited.

### 2.14.3 Brune's Trap Efficiency Curve
The **Trap Efficiency (TE)** of a reservoir = ratio of sediment retained to total sediment inflow.

$$TE = \frac{\text{Sediment retained}}{\text{Sediment inflow}} \times 100$$

Brune's curve relates TE to the **Capacity-Inflow Ratio (C/I)**:

$$\frac{C}{I} = \frac{\text{Reservoir capacity (m}^3\text{)}}{\text{Annual inflow (m}^3\text{)}}$$

For **C/I > 1**, TE ≈ **95-100%**; for **C/I = 0.1**, TE ≈ **30-40%**.

### 2.14.4 Storage Loss in Tanks
- **Annual rate of sedimentation** in Indian tanks/reservoirs: typically **0.5 - 2.0% of storage capacity per year**.
- **CWC (Central Water Commission, India) estimate:** Indian reservoirs lose **~1% storage capacity annually** to siltation.
- **Useful life reduction:** A 100-year design may reduce to **60-80 years** due to siltation.

**Useful life formula:**

$$T = \frac{V}{R_s \cdot \eta}$$

where $T$ = useful life (years), $V$ = original capacity (m³), $R_s$ = annual sediment rate (m³/yr), $\eta$ = trap efficiency (decimal).

# STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivations of USLE Sub-Factors

### 3.1.1 Derivation of Topographic Factor (LS) - Detailed

The **LS-factor** accounts for the combined effect of slope length and steepness on soil loss. It represents the ratio of soil loss from a given slope to that from a standard **22.13 m long, 9% steep slope** (Wischmeier & Smith's standard unit plot).

**Step 1: Start with the fundamental erosion equation**

$$\frac{dA}{dx} = K \cdot R \cdot S_c$$

where $\frac{dA}{dx}$ = soil loss rate per unit area along slope, $S_c$ = local slope gradient (m/m).

**Step 2: Relate soil loss to slope length via steady-state continuity**

At any distance $x$ from the divide, the total sediment load is:

$$A(x) = \int_0^x \frac{dA}{dx} dx$$

**Step 3: Recognize that slope length effect follows a power function**

Based on field data (over 2000 plot-years), Wischmeier established:

$$L = \left(\frac{\lambda}{22.13}\right)^m$$

where $\lambda$ is the actual slope length (m), and exponent $m$ depends on slope gradient ratio.

**Step 4: Slope steepness component**

Empirical relationship based on plot data:

$$S = 0.065 + 0.045 s + 0.0065 s^2$$

where $s$ is slope steepness in percent (%).

**Step 5: Combine as a product**

$$LS = \left(\frac{\lambda}{22.13}\right)^m \times (0.065 + 0.045 s + 0.0065 s^2)$$

**Step 6: Exponent m selection table**

$$m = \begin{cases} 0.2 & \text{if } s < 1\% \\ 0.3 & \text{if } 1\% \leq s < 3\% \\ 0.4 & \text{if } 3\% \leq s < 5\% \\ 0.5 & \text{if } s \geq 5\% \end{cases}$$

**Engineering Utility:** This formula is the basis of **slope-based design** in contour bunding and terracing. Engineers use LS to determine spacing between conservation structures.

### 3.1.2 Derivation of the K-Factor (Wischmeier Nomograph)

**Step 1:** Define the textural parameter

$$M = (\% \text{silt} + \% \text{very fine sand}) \times (100 - \% \text{clay})$$

This M parameter captures the inverse relationship between clay content (binding) and silt+fine-sand content (erodibility).

**Step 2:** Adjust for organic matter

$$M' = M \times (1 - 0.25 \cdot OM)$$

where $OM$ is the fractional organic matter. Organic matter binds soil particles and reduces erodibility; the 0.25 factor is empirically derived.

**Step 3:** Adjust for soil structure code (s)

Structure codes:
- 1 = very fine granular
- 2 = fine granular
- 3 = medium or coarse granular
- 4 = blocky, platy, massive

**Step 4:** Adjust for permeability class (p)

Classes range from 1 (very rapid) to 6 (very slow).

**Step 5:** Combine all into a single regression equation

$$K = 0.1317 \cdot \left[\frac{2.1 \cdot 10^{-4} \cdot M^{1.14}}{100} \cdot (12 - OM) + 3.25 \cdot (s-2) + 2.5 \cdot (p-3) \cdot 10^{-2}\right]$$

**Step 6: Simplification - Standard Wischmeier Formula**

The widely cited form is:

$$K = 2.1 \times 10^{-4} \cdot M^{1.14} \cdot (12 - OM) + 3.25 \times 10^{-2} \cdot (s-2) + 2.5 \times 10^{-3} \cdot (p-3)$$

**Step 7: Numerical Example**

For a soil with: **Silt = 60%, VFS = 10%, Clay = 15%, OM = 2%, s = 2, p = 4**

$$M = (60+10) \times (100-15) = 70 \times 85 = 5950$$

$$K = 2.1 \times 10^{-4} \times (5950)^{1.14} \times (12-2) + 3.25 \times 10^{-2} \times 0 + 2.5 \times 10^{-3} \times 1$$

$$K = 2.1 \times 10^{-4} \times 15814 \times 10 + 0 + 0.0025$$

$$K = 33.21 \times 10^{-4} \times 10 + 0.0025 = 0.0332 + 0.0025 = 0.0357 \text{ t·h/(MJ·mm)}$$

This is a **low-erodibility** soil (typical of fine-textured clayey soils).

### 3.1.3 Derivation of the EI30 R-Factor

**Step 1:** Define kinetic energy per unit depth of rainfall (e):

Empirically (from raindrop data), the unit kinetic energy in MJ/ha·mm is:

$$e = 0.119 + 0.0873 \log_{10} I$$

where $I$ is rainfall intensity in mm/hr.

**Step 2:** Compute kinetic energy of a rainfall segment

If during a time interval $\Delta t$ (hr), the rainfall intensity is $I$ and depth is $\Delta V = I \cdot \Delta t$ (mm), then:

$$KE_{\text{segment}} = e \times \Delta V \text{ (MJ/ha)}$$

**Step 3:** Total storm kinetic energy

$$E_{\text{storm}} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} e_i \cdot \Delta V_i$$

where $n$ is the number of rainfall intensity segments in a storm (typically 5–15 min intervals).

**Step 4:** Determine $I_{30}$

From the storm hyetograph, identify the **maximum rainfall depth in any 30-min period** and convert to intensity (mm/hr).

**Step 5:** Compute EI30 for the storm

$$EI_{30} = E_{\text{storm}} \times I_{30}$$

**Step 6:** Compute annual R-factor

$$R = \sum_{j=1}^{N_{\text{storms}}} (EI_{30})_j$$

where $N_{\text{storms}}$ is the total number of erosive storms in a year.

**Engineering Utility:** The R-factor is used in **regional soil loss mapping** to identify high-erosion-risk zones for prioritizing conservation programs.

### 3.1.4 Worked Numerical Example: Complete USLE Computation

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Problem (KTU Exam Standard):**
> A watershed in Kerala has the following parameters. Estimate the average annual soil loss.
> - R = 600 MJ·mm/(ha·hr·yr)
> - K = 0.30 t·h/(MJ·mm)
> - Slope length = 100 m, slope = 5%
> - C = 0.25 (forest cover)
> - P = 0.5 (contour bunding)

**Step 1: Compute L-factor**

$$L = \left(\frac{\lambda}{22.13}\right)^m = \left(\frac{100}{22.13}\right)^{0.5}$$

$$L = (4.52)^{0.5} = \sqrt{4.52} = 2.13$$

**Step 2: Compute S-factor**

$$S = 0.065 + 0.045 \times 5 + 0.0065 \times 5^2$$

$$S = 0.065 + 0.225 + 0.1625 = 0.4525$$

**Step 3: Compute LS**

$$LS = L \times S = 2.13 \times 0.4525 = 0.964$$

**Step 4: Apply USLE**

$$A = R \times K \times LS \times C \times P$$

$$A = 600 \times 0.30 \times 0.964 \times 0.25 \times 0.5$$

$$A = 600 \times 0.30 \times 0.964 \times 0.125$$

$$A = 600 \times 0.30 \times 0.1205$$

$$A = 600 \times 0.03615$$

$$A = 21.69 \text{ t/ha/yr}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** This watershed loses approximately **21.7 tonnes of soil per hectare per year**. Compare with tolerable soil loss (**T-value**) of **4.5 - 11.2 t/ha/yr** for most soils - **erosion is excessive** and conservation practices need enhancement.

### 3.1.5 Worked Example: EI30 Calculation from Storm Hyetograph

**Problem:** A storm has the following intensity segments (5-min intervals):

| Time (min) | 0-5 | 5-10 | 10-15 | 15-20 | 20-25 | 25-30 |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| Intensity (mm/hr) | 20 | 60 | 100 | 80 | 40 | 10 |
| Depth $\Delta V$ (mm) | 1.67 | 5.0 | 8.33 | 6.67 | 3.33 | 0.83 |

**Step 1: Calculate unit KE for each segment**

Using $e = 0.119 + 0.0873 \log_{10} I$:

$$e_1 = 0.119 + 0.0873 \log_{10}(20) = 0.119 + 0.0873 \times 1.301 = 0.119 + 0.114 = 0.233 \text{ MJ/ha/mm}$$

$$e_2 = 0.119 + 0.0873 \log_{10}(60) = 0.119 + 0.0873 \times 1.778 = 0.119 + 0.155 = 0.274$$

$$e_3 = 0.119 + 0.0873 \log_{10}(100) = 0.119 + 0.0873 \times 2.0 = 0.119 + 0.175 = 0.294$$

$$e_4 = 0.119 + 0.0873 \log_{10}(80) = 0.119 + 0.0873 \times 1.903 = 0.119 + 0.166 = 0.285$$

$$e_5 = 0.119 + 0.0873 \log_{10}(40) = 0.119 + 0.0873 \times 1.602 = 0.119 + 0.140 = 0.259$$

$$e_6 = 0.119 + 0.0873 \log_{10}(10) = 0.119 + 0.0873 \times 1.0 = 0.119 + 0.0873 = 0.206$$

**Step 2: Compute KE for each segment**

$$KE_i = e_i \times \Delta V_i$$

$$KE_1 = 0.233 \times 1.67 = 0.389 \text{ MJ/ha}$$

$$KE_2 = 0.274 \times 5.0 = 1.370$$

$$KE_3 = 0.294 \times 8.33 = 2.449$$

$$KE_4 = 0.285 \times 6.67 = 1.901$$

$$KE_5 = 0.259 \times 3.33 = 0.863$$

$$KE_6 = 0.206 \times 0.83 = 0.171$$

**Step 3: Total storm kinetic energy E**

$$E = \sum KE_i = 0.389 + 1.370 + 2.449 + 1.901 + 0.863 + 0.171 = 7.143 \text{ MJ/ha}$$

**Step 4: Identify I30**

The maximum 30-min intensity is the entire 30-min storm = total depth × 2 = (1.67+5.0+8.33+6.67+3.33+0.83) × 2 = 25.83 × 2 = 51.66 mm/hr

Actually, in 30 minutes the depth is 25.83 mm, so $I_{30} = 25.83 \times 2 = 51.66$ mm/hr.

**Step 5: Compute EI30 for this storm**

$$EI_{30} = 7.143 \times 51.66 = 368.95 \text{ MJ·mm/(ha·hr)}$$

**Step 6: Annual R (if 50 such storms occur in a year)**

$$R = 50 \times 368.95 = 18,447 \text{ MJ·mm/(ha·hr·yr)}$$

This is an extremely high R-value, indicating **very high erosivity climate** (Kerala's western ghats typically have R = 5000-9000).

### 3.1.6 Worked Example: Reservoir Sedimentation & Useful Life

**Problem:** A tank has original capacity $V = 2 \times 10^6$ m³. Annual sediment inflow = 50,000 m³. Trap efficiency $\eta = 80\%$. Find useful life.

**Step 1: Compute annual sediment deposited**

$$R_s = \text{Annual sediment} \times \eta = 50,000 \times 0.80 = 40,000 \text{ m}^3/\text{yr}$$

**Step 2: Compute useful life**

$$T = \frac{V}{R_s} = \frac{2 \times 10^6}{40,000} = 50 \text{ years}$$

**Step 3: Compare with design life**

If design life is 100 years, the tank will lose **half its capacity** in 50 years; effective useful life ≈ 50 years. **Recommendation:** Implement catchment-area conservation measures to extend life.

### 3.1.7 Analytical Matrix: Comparative Summary of Erosion Forms

| Erosion Form | Triggering Agent | Critical Threshold | Detection | Reversibility |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| **Splash** | Raindrop KE | $I > 25$ mm/hr | Visual craters | High (vegetation recovers) |
| **Sheet** | Overland flow | $V < 1$ cm thick | Soil color change | Moderate |
| **Rill** | Concentrated flow | Depth < 30 cm | Visible channels | High (tillage obliterates) |
| **Gully** | Channelized flow | Depth > 30 cm | Aerial photos | Low (structural measures) |
| **Ravine** | Advanced gully | Depth > 3 m | Topographic maps | Very Low |
| **Stream Bank** | Flood flow | Shear > critical | Channel migration | Low |

# KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & RECAP

## PART A QUESTIONS (3 MARKS EACH)

### Question 1: [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]
**Define soil erosion. Differentiate between geological and accelerated erosion with examples.**

**Model Answer:**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition:** Soil erosion is the detachment, transportation, and deposition of soil particles by natural agents such as water, wind, ice, and gravity, leading to a net loss of soil from a given location.

**Geological Erosion:**
- Also called **normal or natural erosion**
- Occurs at a rate **≤ rate of soil formation** (≈ 0.025 - 0.25 mm/year)
- A natural, slow process that is part of the geological cycle
- **Example:** Gradient of mountain slopes under undisturbed forest cover; slow down-wearing of plateaus

**Accelerated Erosion:**
- Occurs at a rate **>> rate of soil formation**
- Primarily caused by **human activities** (deforestation, overgrazing, faulty farming)
- Results in **loss of productivity**, **gully formation**, **reservoir siltation**
- **Example:** Gully erosion in Chambal ravines; siltation of Hirakud reservoir due to deforestation in upper catchment

| Parameter | Geological | Accelerated |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| Rate | Slow | Rapid |
| Cause | Natural | Mostly anthropogenic |
| Effect | Beneficial (renews soil) | Harmful (degrades land) |
| Recovery | Continuous | Requires conservation |

---

### Question 2: [KTU University Exam - May 2024]
**Explain the Universal Soil Loss Equation (USLE) and list its six factors with units.**

**Model Answer:**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **USLE (Wischmeier & Smith, 1965)** is an empirical model that estimates **average annual soil loss (A)** due to sheet and rill erosion from agricultural land.

$$A = R \times K \times L \times S \times C \times P$$

**Six Factors with Units:**

| Factor | Description | Unit |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| **R** | Rainfall erosivity factor | MJ·mm/(ha·hr·yr) |
| **K** | Soil erodibility factor | t·h/(MJ·mm) |
| **L** | Slope length factor | dimensionless |
| **S** | Slope steepness factor | dimensionless |
| **C** | Cover and management factor | dimensionless (0 to 1.5) |
| **P** | Conservation practice factor | dimensionless (0.1 to 1.0) |
| **A** | Average annual soil loss | t/ha/yr |

**Limitations:** USLE predicts only **sheet and rill erosion** (not gully or stream bank); valid for **slopes up to 18%**; does not account for **deposition**.

---

## PART B QUESTIONS (14 MARKS EACH)

### Question A (14 Marks) - Choice 1: [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]

#### (a) Describe the different forms of water erosion with neat sketches. (7 Marks)

**Model Solution:**

The six principal forms of water erosion, in order of increasing severity:

**1. Splash Erosion (Raindrop Erosion):**
- **Mechanism:** Kinetic energy of falling raindrops detaches soil particles
- **Threshold:** Occurs when rainfall intensity > 25 mm/hr
- **Effect:** Soil splash up to 0.5-0.9 m height, soil crusting, surface sealing
- **Sketch:** Small craters with displaced soil; aggregates broken down

**2. Sheet Erosion:**
- **Mechanism:** Uniform removal of a thin layer of soil by overland sheet flow
- **Depth removed:** ~1-2 mm per event (often invisible)
- **Effect:** Most insidious; uniform loss of topsoil and nutrients
- **Sketch:** A broad sloping field with no visible channels

**3. Rill Erosion:**
- **Mechanism:** Concentrated flow forms small, parallel channels (depth < 0.3 m)
- **Effect:** Can be obliterated by normal tillage
- **Sketch:** Small parallel channels along slope

**4. Gully Erosion:**
- **Mechanism:** Advanced rills coalesce into deeper, permanent channels (depth > 0.5 m)
- **Effect:** Cannot be removed by tillage; requires engineering intervention
- **Sketch:** V-shaped or U-shaped deep channel

**5. Ravine Erosion:**
- **Mechanism:** Extreme form of gully with depth > 3 m; forms a network of deep channels
- **Effect:** Converts agricultural land into wasteland (e.g., **Chambal ravines**)
- **Sketch:** Steep-walled, branching deep channels

**6. Stream Bank Erosion:**
- **Mechanism:** Lateral erosion of stream banks by flood waters
- **Effect:** Loss of land along river meanders; bridge foundation threats
- **Sketch:** Curving stream with collapsing outer (cut) bank

#### (b) Explain the five stages of gully development with a diagram. (7 Marks)

**Model Solution:**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Five Classical Stages of Gully Development (Ireland, Schumm & Zimpfer, 1939):**

**Stage 1: Initial Channel Formation**
- Overland flow concentrates in natural depressions
- Small headcut develops at the downslope end
- Vegetation is progressively removed
- Cross-section: shallow, broad, irregular

**Stage 2: Active Channel Development**
- Headcut migrates rapidly upstream (headward erosion)
- Channel deepens and widens
- V-shaped cross-section
- Side walls are near-vertical
- Maximum rate of soil loss

**Stage 3: Headcut Stabilization and Channel Widening**
- Headcut reaches a stable slope gradient (0.5 - 2%)
- Channel widens by sidewall collapse and slumping
- Cross-section transitions to U-shape
- Mass wasting becomes dominant

**Stage 4: Healing Phase**
- Slope gradient becomes gentler
- Vegetation re-establishes on sidewalls
- Soil creep and sloughing reduce channel depth
- Cross-section broadens further

**Stage 5: Equilibrium / Graded Profile**
- Channel reaches a graded profile (no further net erosion)
- Vegetation fully established
- Channel acts as a stable drainageway
- Will only erode under extreme events

> [!NOTE]
> **Preventive Measures:** Gully control is most effective in **Stage 1 and 2** (when headcut is forming). Once a gully reaches **Stage 3**, it is essentially irreversible without major engineering works (check dams, drop structures, vegetation establishment).

---

### Question B (14 Marks) - Choice 2: [KTU University Exam - May 2024]

#### (a) With neat sketches, explain the EI30 method of computing the rainfall erosivity factor R. (7 Marks)

**Model Solution:**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **EI30 Method (Wischmeier & Smith, 1958, modified 1978):**
> The product of the **total storm kinetic energy (E)** and the **maximum 30-minute rainfall intensity (I30)**.

**Step 1: Define Kinetic Energy of Rainfall**
The kinetic energy per unit depth of rainfall is an empirical function of intensity:

$$e = 0.119 + 0.0873 \log_{10} I \text{ (MJ/ha·mm)}$$

where $I$ = rainfall intensity (mm/hr)

**Step 2: Calculate E (Total Storm Kinetic Energy)**
Divide the storm hyetograph into time segments (typically 5-15 min intervals). For each segment, calculate $e_i$ from intensity, then:

$$KE_i = e_i \times \Delta V_i$$

Sum to get total storm energy:

$$E = \sum_{i=1}^{n} e_i \cdot \Delta V_i \text{ (MJ/ha)}$$

**Step 3: Determine I30**
Identify the **maximum rainfall depth in any continuous 30-minute period** from the storm hyetograph, then convert to intensity:

$$I_{30} = \frac{\text{Max 30-min depth (mm)}}{0.5 \text{ hr}} \text{ (mm/hr)}$$

**Step 4: Compute EI30 for the Storm**

$$EI_{30} = E \times I_{30} \text{ (MJ·mm/(ha·hr))}$$

**Step 5: Sum Annual R-Factor**

$$R = \sum_{j=1}^{N} (EI_{30})_j \text{ (MJ·mm/(ha·hr·yr))}$$

**Sketch Description:** Show a hyetograph with intensity vs. time, identify the 30-min window with maximum intensity (I30), and indicate the energy computation regions.

**Advantages of EI30 over KE>25:**
- Includes all erosive storms (not just those > 25 mm/hr)
- Better correlates with measured soil loss (R² ≈ 0.85)
- Standardized globally for USLE applications

#### (b) A watershed has R = 700 MJ·mm/(ha·hr·yr), K = 0.25 t·h/(MJ·mm), slope length = 80 m, slope = 4%, C = 0.2, P = 0.4. Compute the annual soil loss using USLE. Comment on the result. (7 Marks)

**Model Solution:**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Given:**
> - R = 700 MJ·mm/(ha·hr·yr)
> - K = 0.25 t·h/(MJ·mm)
> - $\lambda$ = 80 m, s = 4%
> - C = 0.2 (forest/scrub cover)
> - P = 0.4 (contour bunding)

**Step 1: Compute L-factor**

$$L = \left(\frac{\lambda}{22.13}\right)^m = \left(\frac{80}{22.13}\right)^m$$

For slope = 4%, exponent $m$ = 0.4 (since 3% ≤ s < 5%):

$$L = (3.615)^{0.4} = e^{0.4 \times \ln(3.615)} = e^{0.4 \times 1.285} = e^{0.514} = 1.672$$

**Step 2: Compute S-factor**

$$S = 0.065 + 0.045 s + 0.0065 s^2$$

$$S = 0.065 + 0.045 \times 4 + 0.0065 \times 16 = 0.065 + 0.18 + 0.104 = 0.349$$

**Step 3: Compute LS**

$$LS = L \times S = 1.672 \times 0.349 = 0.5835$$

**Step 4: Apply USLE**

$$A = R \times K \times LS \times C \times P$$

$$A = 700 \times 0.25 \times 0.5835 \times 0.2 \times 0.4$$

$$A = 700 \times 0.25 \times 0.5835 \times 0.08$$

$$A = 700 \times 0.01167$$

$$A = 8.169 \text{ t/ha/yr}$$

**Step 5: Comment on the Result**

- The estimated soil loss is **8.17 t/ha/yr**
- **Tolerable soil loss (T-value):** Typically **4.5 - 11.2 t/ha/yr** depending on soil depth and rooting
- The result is **within the tolerable range** but **approaching the upper limit**
- **Recommendations:**
  1. Maintain or improve vegetation cover (reduce C further)
  2. Implement additional conservation practices (terracing)
  3. Monitor soil loss regularly
  4. Reassess after major land-use changes

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning:**
> - **Common Mistake #1:** Students often forget to use the **correct exponent m** for slope length based on slope %. Forgetting this leads to wrong L-factor and **5-7 mark loss**.
> - **Common Mistake #2:** Unit inconsistency in R. Always ensure R is in **MJ·mm/(ha·hr·yr)** and K in **t·h/(MJ·mm)** to get A in **t/ha/yr**.
> - **Common Mistake #3:** Forgetting to **comment on the result** in part (b) - this carries 1-2 marks.
> - **Common Mistake #4:** Using L and S factors from memory without rederiving them from the formulas - if values are not standard, this leads to wrong answers.

---

# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Wind Erosion – Formal Definition

**Wind Erosion** is the detachment, transportation, and deposition of soil particles by the kinetic energy of wind. It is a major form of soil degradation in arid, semi-arid, and dry sub-humid regions where vegetative cover is sparse, soil is dry, and wind velocity exceeds a critical threshold.

According to the KTU 2024 scheme, wind erosion is classified as a **three-phase process** involving:
1. **Detachment** of soil aggregates from the soil mass
2. **Transportation** of detached particles by aerodynamic lift and drag forces
3. **Deposition** when wind velocity falls below the threshold for the particle size

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Wind erosion is significant in India across the states of **Rajasthan, Gujarat, Haryana, and parts of Punjab and Andhra Pradesh**. The annual soil loss from wind erosion in India is estimated at approximately **5330 million tonnes**.

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine blowing across a dusty table. Light dust particles fly into the air and travel a long distance (this is **suspension**), heavier grains bounce and hop along the surface (**saltation**), and the largest grains roll along the table (**surface creep**). Wind erosion in fields works exactly the same way, but with **trillions** of soil particles and with enough energy to strip the topsoil of an entire farm in a single dust storm.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** Wind erosion does NOT need steep slopes. Unlike water erosion, wind erosion is most aggressive on **flat, dry, loose, and bare soils** — which is why desertification is its worst consequence.

## 1.2 Water Erosion Control Measures – Formal Definition

**Water erosion control measures** are engineering and agronomic practices designed to reduce the detachment and transport capacity of flowing water on agricultural and non-agricultural lands. They are broadly classified into:

- **Agronomical Measures** (biological/vegetative): Contour farming, strip cropping, conservation tillage, mulching
- **Engineering Measures** (structural): Bunds, terraces, contour stone walls, trenches

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Think of rainwater on a sloped field as a **racing car without brakes**. **Agronomical measures** are like the **driver's careful steering** along safe contour paths (slowing it down), while **engineering measures** are the **physical crash barriers** (bunds, terraces) that stop the car. Both are needed; the agronomic measures reduce the speed, and the engineering measures stop the vehicle entirely.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Board Pattern Note:** A typical 14-mark question will ask you to "design a bund/terrace system for a given slope and rainfall." Mastery of design formulas (VI, bund cross-section) is mandatory.

## 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

| Parameter | Standard Value / Unit |
|---|---|
| Threshold wind velocity for erosion | **6.0 m/s at 1 m height** (for dry, loose, fine sand) |
| Critical wind velocity for damage | **> 8.0 m/s sustained** |
| Shelter belt porosity (optimum) | **40 to 60%** |
| Shelter belt protection (leeward) | **10 to 15 × Tree Height (H)** |
| Shelter belt protection (windward) | **2 to 5 × Tree Height (H)** |
| Contour bund spacing (0–6% slope) | **Vertical Interval (VI) = 0.3 m** |
| Graded bund grade (general) | **0.2% to 0.4%** |

# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Wind Erosion – Factors Affecting

### A. Climatic Factors
1. **Wind Velocity** – Erosion increases **exponentially** (cubically) with wind speed. Doubling wind speed can increase erosion 8 to 64 times.
2. **Wind Direction** – Prevailing winds determine orientation of shelter belts.
3. **Wind Duration** – Longer-duration winds cause cumulative damage.
4. **Air Humidity & Rainfall** – Moist soil resists detachment. Rainfall < 250 mm/year → high wind erosion zones.
5. **Temperature** – Influences evaporation and thus soil moisture.

### B. Soil Factors
1. **Soil Texture** – **Silt-sized particles (0.05–0.10 mm)** are most susceptible because they are easily lifted yet large enough to cause abrasion.
2. **Soil Structure** – Granular/crumb structure is more resistant than single-grain structure.
3. **Soil Moisture** – Higher moisture → higher threshold velocity (capillary forces bind particles).
4. **Surface Crust** – Stable crusts (e.g., desert pavement, biological soil crusts) protect underlying soil.
5. **Soil Density & Organic Matter** – Higher density and organic matter increase resistance.

### C. Vegetative Cover
- Even **25–30% cover** reduces erosion by ~50%.
- Bare soil (0% cover) → maximum erosion.

### D. Topographic Factors
- Length of field (unsheltered distance) – longer fields = higher erosion.
- Topography affects wind turbulence and acceleration.

### E. Cultural / Management Factors
- Tillage, crop residue management, overgrazing.

## 2.2 Mechanics of Wind Erosion (Three Modes of Particle Movement)

### Mode 1: **Saltation** (50–75% of total transport)
- Particles **0.1 to 0.5 mm** in size.
- Lifted to heights of **< 1 m** (low saltation) or up to several meters (high saltation).
- Travel in **ballistic trajectories** — they hop, strike the surface, dislodge other particles.
- This is the **most important** mechanism because it triggers surface creep and suspension.

### Mode 2: **Suspension** (< 25% of total transport by mass, but travels farthest)
- Particles **< 0.1 mm** (silt and clay).
- Carried by **turbulent eddies** to great heights (hundreds of meters) and distances (hundreds of km).
- Forms **dust storms** and contributes to **red rain, haze, and loess deposits**.

### Mode 3: **Surface Creep** (5–25% of total transport)
- Particles **0.5 to 2.0 mm** in size.
- Too heavy to lift, too coarse to saltate.
- **Roll, slide, or creep** along the surface due to impact of saltating particles.
- Causes abrasion of vegetation and infrastructure.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Memory Trick:** **S**uspension = **S**mallest particles, **S**ky-high travel. **C**reep = **C**oarsest particles, **C**rawl on ground. **S**altation = **S**and-sized, **S**ignature mechanism.

## 2.3 Soil Loss Estimation – Wind Erosion Equation (WEQ)

### Original Wind Erosion Equation (WEQ) by Woodruff & Siddoway (1965)

$$
E = f(I, K, C, L, V)
$$

Where:
- **E** = Potential average annual soil loss (tonnes/ha/year)
- **I** = **Soil erodibility index** (depends on texture, structure, etc.)
- **K** = **Soil ridge roughness factor**
- **C** = **Climatic factor** (depends on wind velocity and surface moisture)
- **L** = **Unsheltered distance** (field length along prevailing wind, m)
- **V** = **Vegetative cover factor**

### Product Form (Chepil-Milne modified):

$$
E = I \times K \times C \times L \times V
$$

### Revised Wind Erosion Equation (RWEQ) – Modern Version

$$
E = Q_{max} \cdot e^{-\left( \dfrac{z}{z_0} \right)^h}
$$

Where:
- $Q_{max}$ = maximum transport capacity (kg/m width)
- $z$ = cumulative distance covered by saltation flow
- $z_0$ = decay coefficient (depends on surface roughness)
- $h$ = shape factor

> [!NOTE]
> **For KTU 2024 Exam:** The classic multiplicative WEQ form is most commonly asked. Memorize the five factors I, K, C, L, V.

### KTU Formula Sheet (Master Cheat Table)

| Formula | Expression | Use / Application |
|---|---|---|
| Wind Erosion Equation | $E = I \cdot K \cdot C \cdot L \cdot V$ | Annual soil loss prediction |
| Threshold Friction Velocity | $U_{t}^{\ast} = A \sqrt{\frac{\rho_s - \rho}{\rho} \cdot g \cdot d}$ | Minimum wind to move particle of diameter *d* |
| Shelter Belt Protection (Leeward) | $L = 15 H$ to $25 H$ | Wind reduction zone |
| Shelter Belt Height | $H$ in metres | Used in spacing calculations |
| Contour Bund VI (slope < 6%) | $VI = 0.3 \text{ m}$ | Vertical Interval |
| Contour Bund VI (slope 6–10%) | $VI = 0.3 + (S - 6) \times 0.06$ | Adjusted VI |
| Graded Bund Length | $L_b = 0.4 \text{ to } 1.0 \text{ km}$ | Maximum length |
| Graded Bund Grade | $g = 0.2\% \text{ to } 0.4\%$ | Channel slope |
| Terrace Earthwork | $V_{cut} = V_{fill} = \frac{L \cdot A^2}{8}$ | Per running metre |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pipe Symbol Trap:** Never use `|` (vertical bar) inside table cells for absolute values. Use $\vert$ or $\mid$ in LaTeX to avoid breaking the markdown table.

### Real-World Utility

- **WEQ** is used globally by USDA-NRCS for designing shelter belts, residue management, and predicting dust storm intensity.
- **RWEQ** is used in modern GIS-based erosion modeling (e.g., for assessing climate change impacts on drylands).

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Detailed Derivation: WEQ Components and their Roles

### Step 1: Soil Erodibility (I) – Determination

Soil erodibility (I) is determined from a nomograph using:
- **% of dry soil aggregates > 0.84 mm**
- **% of dry soil aggregates < 0.42 mm**
- **% of organic matter**
- **% of CaCO₃ (calcium carbonate)**
- **Soil texture class**

The relationship is empirically fitted as:

$$
I = f(\text{texture, structure, OM, CaCO}_3)
$$

Typical values:
- Sandy soils: $I = 50$ to $100$ (high erodibility)
- Clayey soils: $I = 10$ to $30$ (low erodibility)

### Step 2: Soil Ridge Roughness (K)

K is a function of ridge height and spacing:

$$
K = \frac{\text{Ridge height (cm)}}{25.4} \cdot f(\text{ridge orientation to wind})
$$

For ridges parallel to wind: $K = 1.0$ (no protection)
For ridges perpendicular to wind: $K = 0.5$ (max protection)

### Step 3: Climatic Factor (C)

$$
C = 1.0 - \frac{P}{I_a}
$$

Where:
- $P$ = daily rainfall (mm)
- $I_a$ = antecedent precipitation index

Higher rainfall → higher C reduction → less wind erosion. Dry years → C = 1.0.

### Step 4: Unsheltered Distance (L)

$$
L = \frac{\text{Field length along prevailing wind (m)}}{100}
$$

The longer the field in wind direction, the higher the cumulative erosion.

### Step 5: Vegetative Cover (V)

For flat residues (stubble):

$$
V = e^{-0.00088 \cdot M_r}
$$

Where $M_r$ = residue mass (kg/ha). 

For standing residues, V is reduced further (more effective).

> [!NOTE]
> **Reasoning Note:** The exponential form arises because each unit of residue multiplies a fixed protection fraction — the classic "law of diminishing returns" applied to vegetative cover.

## 3.2 Worked Example – WEQ Calculation

**Problem:** A 500 m long field has clay loam soil (I = 30), perpendicular ridges of 15 cm height (K = 0.6), annual climatic factor C = 0.85, and 3000 kg/ha of flat residue. Compute annual soil loss.

**Given:**
- $I = 30$
- $K = 0.6$
- $C = 0.85$
- $L = 500/100 = 5$
- $M_r = 3000$ kg/ha

**Step 1: Compute V**

$$
V = e^{-0.00088 \times 3000} = e^{-2.64}
$$

$$
V = 0.0714
$$

**Step 2: Apply WEQ**

$$
E = I \times K \times C \times L \times V
$$

$$
E = 30 \times 0.6 \times 0.85 \times 5 \times 0.0714
$$

$$
E = 30 \times 0.6 = 18
$$

$$
E = 18 \times 0.85 = 15.3
$$

$$
E = 15.3 \times 5 = 76.5
$$

$$
E = 76.5 \times 0.0714 = 5.46 \text{ tonnes/ha/year}
$$

**Result:** Annual soil loss ≈ **5.46 tonnes/ha/year**.

## 3.3 Derivation: Threshold Friction Velocity

The **threshold friction velocity** ($U_t^*$) is the minimum wind shear velocity needed to move a soil particle. Bagnold (1941) derived it as:

$$
U_t^{\ast} = A \cdot \sqrt{\frac{\rho_s - \rho}{\rho} \cdot g \cdot d}
$$

Where:
- $A$ = empirical constant ≈ 0.1 for particles > 0.1 mm
- $\rho_s$ = particle density (≈ 2650 kg/m³ for quartz)
- $\rho$ = air density (≈ 1.225 kg/m³)
- $g$ = gravitational acceleration (9.81 m/s²)
- $d$ = particle diameter (m)

**Numerical Example:** For $d = 0.0005$ m (0.5 mm sand):

$$
U_t^{\ast} = 0.1 \cdot \sqrt{\frac{2650 - 1.225}{1.225} \cdot 9.81 \cdot 0.0005}
$$

$$
U_t^{\ast} = 0.1 \cdot \sqrt{2163.55 \cdot 9.81 \cdot 0.0005}
$$

$$
U_t^{\ast} = 0.1 \cdot \sqrt{10.61}
$$

$$
U_t^{\ast} = 0.1 \cdot 3.258 = 0.326 \text{ m/s}
$$

Corresponding wind velocity at 1 m height ≈ **5.8 m/s**, matching field observations.

## 3.4 Derivation: Vertical Interval (VI) for Bunds

The **Vertical Interval** between successive contour bunds is given by the ICAR formula:

For slopes **< 6%**:

$$
VI = 0.3 \text{ m}
$$

For slopes **6% to 10%**:

$$
VI = 0.3 + (S - 6) \cdot 0.06
$$

For slopes **> 10%**:

$$
VI = 0.3 + (S - 6) \cdot 0.10
$$

Where $S$ is the land slope in percent.

### Worked Example

**Problem:** A field has 8% slope. Design the VI.

$$
VI = 0.3 + (8 - 6) \cdot 0.06
$$

$$
VI = 0.3 + 0.12 = 0.42 \text{ m}
$$

**Horizontal Interval (HI):**

$$
HI = \frac{VI}{S} \times 100 = \frac{0.42}{8} \times 100 = 5.25 \text{ m}
$$

## 3.5 Derivation: Bund Cross-Section Design

For a trapezoidal bund with side slopes **m:1** (m = 1.5 to 2.0 for earth bunds):

- **Top width** (T) = 0.3 to 0.5 m
- **Side slopes** (m) = 1.5H:1V to 2H:1V
- **Freeboard** = 0.1 to 0.15 m

### Cross-sectional area:

$$
A = \frac{1}{2} \cdot (T + B) \cdot H_b
$$

Where:
- $B$ = bottom width = $T + 2mH_b$
- $H_b$ = bund height

Therefore:

$$
A = \frac{1}{2} \cdot (T + T + 2mH_b) \cdot H_b
$$

$$
A = \frac{1}{2} \cdot (2T + 2mH_b) \cdot H_b
$$

$$
A = (T + mH_b) \cdot H_b
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Board Examiner Note:** In KTU exams, if the design rainfall is given, the bund height $H_b$ is determined from the runoff depth:

$$
H_b = \text{runoff depth} + \text{freeboard}
$$

$$
H_b = \frac{R \cdot C_r}{1000} + 0.15
$$

Where $R$ = 24-hr max rainfall (mm) and $C_r$ = runoff coefficient (0.3 to 0.5 for arable land).

## PART A QUESTIONS (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define Wind Erosion. List the three modes of particle movement in wind erosion. [KTU University Exam – May 2023]

**Model Answer:**

**Wind Erosion:** It is the detachment, transportation, and deposition of soil particles caused by wind action, primarily affecting dry, bare soils in arid and semi-arid regions.

**Three Modes of Particle Movement:**

1. **Saltation:** Sand-sized particles (0.1 to 0.5 mm) move in a series of bounces along the surface. They travel in ballistic trajectories and dislodge other particles upon impact. Accounts for **50–75%** of total transport.

2. **Suspension:** Fine particles (< 0.1 mm, silt and clay) are carried aloft by turbulent eddies, often to heights of several hundred meters and distances of hundreds of kilometres. Forms dust storms and contributes to loess deposits.

3. **Surface Creep:** Coarse particles (0.5 to 2.0 mm) roll, slide, or creep along the surface due to the impact energy of saltating grains. Accounts for 5–25% of total transport.

> [!NOTE]
> **Marks Distribution (KTU Pattern):** Definition – 1 mark, Three modes – 1.5 marks (0.5 each), Brief explanation – 0.5 marks.

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Contour Bund | Graded Bund |
|---|---|---|
| **Alignment** | Exactly along contour (zero grade) | Slight grade (0.2 to 0.4%) along contour |
| **Drainage** | Water stagnates between bunds | Water drained safely through grassed waterway |
| **Use** | In low-rainfall areas (< 750 mm/yr) | In high-rainfall areas (> 750 mm/yr) |
| **Failure Risk** | Bund may breach if overtopped | Safe disposal prevents breach |

## PART B QUESTIONS (14 Marks Each)

### Question A (14 Marks) – Wind Erosion Estimation and Control [KTU University Exam – May 2024]

**Q. A (a)** Explain the factors affecting wind erosion in detail. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

Wind erosion is influenced by **five major groups of factors:**

**(1) Climatic Factors:**
- **Wind Velocity:** Most important. Erosion increases cubically with wind speed. Threshold velocity for dry, fine sand is ~6 m/s at 1 m height.
- **Wind Direction:** Prevailing wind direction dictates orientation of control measures.
- **Wind Duration:** Sustained high winds cause greater cumulative damage.
- **Humidity and Rainfall:** Higher humidity and rainfall increase soil cohesion, reducing erodibility.
- **Temperature:** Influences evaporation rate and hence soil moisture.

**(2) Soil Factors:**
- **Texture:** Silt-sized particles (0.05–0.10 mm) are most erodible.
- **Structure:** Granular structure resists detachment more than single-grain.
- **Moisture:** Wet soil requires higher threshold velocity due to capillary forces.
- **Surface Crust:** Stable crusts (e.g., desert pavement) protect underlying soil.
- **Organic Matter and Density:** Higher values increase resistance.

**(3) Vegetative Cover:**
- Even 25–30% cover can reduce erosion by half.
- Roots bind soil; stems obstruct wind flow at ground level.

**(4) Topographic Factors:**
- Longer unsheltered distance = more erosion.
- Topographic features create wind tunnels and turbulence.

**(5) Cultural / Management Factors:**
- Tillage direction (perpendicular ridges reduce erosion by ~50%).
- Crop residue management (residue retention reduces erosion exponentially).
- Overgrazing exposes soil to wind action.

> [!NOTE]
> **Board Pattern (7 marks):** List 5 factors – 2.5 marks (0.5 each), explanation of each – 4.5 marks (0.9 each).

**Q. A (b)** Write the Wind Erosion Equation (WEQ) and explain each factor in detail. Compute the annual soil loss for a 400 m long field with: I = 40, K = 0.7, C = 0.9, and 2000 kg/ha flat residue. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Wind Erosion Equation:**

$$
E = f(I, K, C, L, V) = I \times K \times C \times L \times V
$$

**Explanation of Factors:**

- **I (Soil Erodibility Index):** Inherent susceptibility of soil to wind erosion, based on texture, structure, organic matter, and CaCO₃ content. Determined from nomograph. Range: 10 (clay) to 100 (sand).

- **K (Soil Ridge Roughness):** Accounts for surface roughness created by tillage. Ridges perpendicular to wind reduce erosion. K = 0.5 (perpendicular) to 1.0 (parallel).

- **C (Climatic Factor):** Reflects wind velocity, duration, and soil moisture. Higher in dry windy regions, lower in humid regions. Range: 0 to 1.

- **L (Unsheltered Distance):** Field length along prevailing wind direction. L = field length (m) / 100.

- **V (Vegetative Cover Factor):** Reduces erosion exponentially. Higher with crop residue. Computed as $V = e^{-0.00088 M_r}$ for flat residue.

**Numerical Solution:**

**Given:**
- I = 40
- K = 0.7
- C = 0.9
- Field length = 400 m → L = 400/100 = 4
- M_r = 2000 kg/ha

**Step 1: Compute V**

$$
V = e^{-0.00088 \times 2000} = e^{-1.76}
$$

$$
V = 0.1720
$$

**Step 2: Apply WEQ**

$$
E = 40 \times 0.7 \times 0.9 \times 4 \times 0.1720
$$

$$
E = 40 \times 0.7 = 28
$$

$$
E = 28 \times 0.9 = 25.2
$$

$$
E = 25.2 \times 4 = 100.8
$$

$$
E = 100.8 \times 0.1720 = 17.34 \text{ tonnes/ha/year}
$$

**Result:** Annual soil loss ≈ **17.34 tonnes/ha/year**.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfall:** Students often forget to divide field length by 100 to get L. KTU examiners specifically check this step. **2 marks are deducted** if L is kept as 400.

### Question B (14 Marks) – Bund and Terrace Design [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

**Q. B (a)** Design a contour bund system for a 2.5 ha rectangular field (100 m × 250 m) on a 5% slope in a sub-humid region receiving 750 mm annual rainfall. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Given:**
- Field area: 2.5 ha = 25,000 m²
- Dimensions: 100 m × 250 m
- Slope: S = 5%
- Rainfall: 750 mm/year
- Region: Sub-humid (use graded bunds due to high rainfall)

**Step 1: Vertical Interval (VI) for slope < 6%**

$$
VI = 0.3 \text{ m}
$$

**Step 2: Horizontal Interval (HI)**

$$
HI = \frac{VI}{S} \times 100 = \frac{0.3}{5} \times 100 = 6.0 \text{ m}
$$

**Step 3: Number of Bunds**

Along the slope (250 m side), number of intervals = 250 / 6 = 41.67 → 42 intervals.

Number of bunds = 42 - 1 = **41 bunds** (since topmost bund is at the boundary).

**Step 4: Bund Cross-Section**

Assume a 24-hour max rainfall of 100 mm and runoff coefficient $C_r = 0.4$:

$$
\text{Runoff depth} = R \times C_r = 100 \times 0.4 = 40 \text{ mm} = 0.04 \text{ m}
$$

**Step 5: Bund Height (H_b)**

$$
H_b = 0.04 + 0.10 = 0.14 \text{ m} \text{ (runoff + freeboard)}
$$

**Step 6: Bottom Width**

Side slope m = 1.5, top width T = 0.3 m:

$$
B = T + 2mH_b = 0.3 + 2(1.5)(0.14) = 0.3 + 0.42 = 0.72 \text{ m}
$$

**Step 7: Cross-Sectional Area**

$$
A = (T + mH_b) \cdot H_b = (0.3 + 1.5 \times 0.14) \times 0.14
$$

$$
A = (0.3 + 0.21) \times 0.14 = 0.51 \times 0.14 = 0.0714 \text{ m}^2
$$

**Step 8: Total Earthwork**

Length of each bund = 100 m, total length = 41 × 100 = 4100 m

$$
\text{Total volume} = A \times \text{Total Length} = 0.0714 \times 4100 = 292.74 \text{ m}^3
$$

**Result:**
- Number of bunds: **41**
- Bund height: **0.14 m**
- Bottom width: **0.72 m**
- Total earthwork: **292.74 m³**

**Q. B (b)** Explain the planning, design, and layout procedure of bench terraces. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**1. Definition:** Bench terraces are a series of step-like platforms constructed across the slope, transforming a sloping field into a series of level or nearly level benches separated by vertical or near-vertical risers.

**2. Planning Procedure:**

**(a) Topographic Survey:**
- Prepare a contour map at 0.5 to 1.0 m vertical interval.
- Identify slope class: < 6% (not needed), 6–16% (broad base), 16–33% (bench), > 33% (not recommended).

**(b) Layout of Center Line:**
- The first (uppermost) terrace is laid out on the contour at a pre-determined elevation.
- Subsequent terraces are laid out by measuring Vertical Interval (VI) along the slope.

**(c) Selection of Terrace Type:**
- **Table-top (level)** bench: for permeable soils and low rainfall.
- **Inward-sloping (graded)** bench: for high rainfall and low-permeability soils.
- **Outward-sloping bench:** rarely used (causes erosion at riser).

**3. Design Procedure:**

**(a) Vertical Interval (VI):**

For bench terraces in India (Khanna's formula):

$$
VI = H_b \times (1 + S/H_b) \cdot 0.5 \cdot 1/S
$$

Simplified ICAR formula:

$$
VI = 0.6 \text{ to } 1.2 \text{ m (typical)}
$$

**(b) Riser Height ($H_b$):** Equal to VI for level terraces.

**(c) Bench Width (W):**

For level bench terraces:

$$
W = \frac{VI}{S} - H_b
$$

For graded bench terraces (allowing channel width $C_w$):

$$
W = \frac{VI}{S} - H_b - C_w
$$

**(d) Riser Slope:** Usually 1:1 or 0.5:1 (vertical:horizontal).

**(e) Cross-Section of Channel (for graded):**

Trapezoidal: bottom width 0.3 m, depth 0.15 m, side slopes 1.5:1.

**4. Layout Procedure:**

(i) Start at the **top of the field** and lay out the first terrace.
(ii) Measure **VI vertically** down the slope using an Abney level or dumpy level.
(iii) Mark the next contour.
(iv) Stake out the **cut line and fill line** along the contour.
(v) Excavate soil from the upper half of the terrace and dump on the lower half to form a level bench.
(vi) Stabilize the riser with stones, grass, or geotextile.

**5. Earthwork Calculation:**

$$
V_{cut} = V_{fill} = \frac{1}{8} \cdot \frac{A^2 \cdot L}{100}
$$

Where A = cross-sectional area (m²) and L = terrace length (m).

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Warning:** Students commonly confuse **contour bunds** (used for slope < 6% in low rainfall) with **bench terraces** (used for slope > 16%). Examiners allocate 1–2 marks specifically for correct selection criteria. Do NOT mix them.

---

## 5.3 Contour Stonewalls and Trenching (Brief Notes)

### Contour Stone Walls
- **Use:** Rocky/stony soils where bunds are not feasible.
- **Construction:** Dry stone masonry, 0.5 to 1.0 m height, on contour.
- **Spacing:** Vertical Interval (VI) = 0.5 to 1.0 m depending on slope.
- **Function:** Intercepts runoff, traps sediments, recharges groundwater.

### Trenching
- **Use:** Moisture conservation in dryland farming.
- **Types:** **Continuous contour trenches (CCT)** and **staggered trenches**.
- **Dimensions:** 0.3 m wide × 0.3 m deep, on contour.
- **Spacing:** VI = 2 to 5 m depending on slope.
- **Function:** Harvests runoff, increases infiltration, supports tree planting.

### Trench Design Formula

$$
\text{Storage volume per unit length} = b \times d
$$

Where $b$ = trench width (m) and $d$ = trench depth (m).

For a 30% slope, design VI:

$$
VI = 2.0 \text{ m}, \text{ spacing} = \frac{2.0}{0.30} = 6.67 \text{ m horizontal}
$$

---

## 5.4 KTU 2024 Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Revision Points for KTU 2024 Board Exam:**

### Wind Erosion
- ✅ Three modes: **Saltation, Suspension, Surface Creep** (sizes: 0.1–0.5 mm, < 0.1 mm, 0.5–2.0 mm).
- ✅ **WEQ:** $E = I \times K \times C \times L \times V$
- ✅ Threshold wind velocity at 1 m height = **6 m/s**.
- ✅ Shelter belt porosity optimum = **40 to 60%**.
- ✅ Protection zone: leeward = **15H**, windward = **5H**.
- ✅ Sand dune stabilization methods: brushwood check dams, sand fences, revegetation.
- ✅ Factors affecting wind erosion: climatic, soil, vegetative, topographic, cultural.

### Water Erosion – Agronomical Measures
- ✅ **Contour farming:** tillage across slope along contour.
- ✅ **Strip cropping:** alternating strips of erosion-permitting and erosion-resisting crops.
- ✅ **Conservation tillage:** minimum tillage with residue retention.
- ✅ **Mulching:** covering soil with organic/inorganic material to reduce splash erosion.

### Water Erosion – Engineering Measures
- ✅ **Contour Bunds:** VI = 0.3 m for slopes < 6%.
- ✅ **Graded Bunds:** grade = 0.2 to 0.4%.
- ✅ **Broad Base Terraces:** level or graded; for 6 to 16% slopes.
- ✅ **Bench Terraces:** for 16 to 33% slopes; riser, bench width, VI formulas.
- ✅ **Contour Stonewall:** for stony soils; VI = 0.5 to 1.0 m.
- ✅ **Trenching:** CCT and staggered; depth 0.3 m × width 0.3 m.

### High-Yield Formulas to Memorize
- ✅ $VI = 0.3 + (S - 6) \times 0.06$ for 6% < S < 10%
- ✅ $A = (T + mH_b) \cdot H_b$ for bund cross-section
- ✅ $V = e^{-0.00088 M_r}$ for vegetative cover factor
- ✅ $W = \frac{VI}{S} - H_b$ for bench terrace width

### Common KTU Board Traps
- ⚠️ Forgetting to convert field length to L (divide by 100) in WEQ.
- ⚠️ Confusing contour bunds with graded bunds (use the rainfall/slope criteria).
- ⚠️ Mixing broad base terraces (6–16%) with bench terraces (>16%).
- ⚠️ Using absolute value pipes `|` in markdown tables (use $\vert$ or $\mid$).

# MODULE 3: GULLY & RAVINE RECLAMATION AND PERMANENT STRUCTURES

## 1.1 GULLY EROSION — FUNDAMENTAL CONCEPTS

### 1.1.1 Formal Academic Definition
**Gully erosion** is the advanced stage of surface soil erosion in which concentrated runoff removes soil in discrete, deep channels (gullies) that cannot be obliterated by ordinary tillage operations. Once a gully forms, it enlarges through three processes: **headward erosion** (upstream migration of the gully head), **sidewall sloughing**, and **downward piping/channel deepening**. A **ravine** is essentially a network of deep, wide, and often permanently established gullies, typically found along riverbanks or in deeply weathered alluvial soils.

> [!NOTE]
> **Classification of Gullies (USDA Soil Conservation Service):**
> - **Small Gully** — Cross-sectional area < 0.5 m², depth < 1 m
> - **Medium Gully** — Cross-sectional area 0.5–5.0 m²
> - **Large Gully** — Cross-sectional area > 5.0 m²
> - **Ravine** — Deep, wide gully with vertical or near-vertical sidewalls

### 1.1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Wound on the Land"
Imagine a flat field as human skin. Sheet erosion is like a mild scrape — it heals easily. A gully is a deep cut or wound. Once the cut is made, every drop of water that flows through it deepens the wound further (headward erosion), the wound's edges crumble and fall in (sidewall erosion), and the body's natural healing mechanisms (vegetation) cannot bridge the gap without external sutures. **Gully control measures are the "stitches" and "bandages"** that stop bleeding, allow healing, and restore function to the land.

### 1.1.3 Stages of Gully Development
Gullies evolve through a predictable sequence:

1. **Sheet & Rill Stage** — Uniform surface removal
2. **Gully Channel Initiation** — Concentrated flow forms discontinuous channels
3. **Headcut Migration Stage** — Vertical fall at the gully head retreats upstream
4. **Widening & Stabilization** — Sidewalls slump, then partially stabilize
5. **Healing Stage** — Vegetation establishes if flow energy is reduced

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Headward Erosion** advances at rates of **0.3 to 3 m per year** in unprotected conditions. Each meter of headcut retreat can cause **15–50 m² of land loss** from the gully margins.

## 1.2 PRINCIPLES OF GULLY CONTROL

The control strategy follows a **Three-Tier Hierarchy**:

### 1.2.1 Principle 1 — Runoff Control at Source
Reduce the volume and velocity of water entering the gully by diverting upstream runoff through **diversion drains**, **contour bunds**, and **terraces**.

### 1.2.2 Principle 2 — In-Channel Energy Dissipation
Reduce the erosive force of water already in the gully by **check dams**, **drop structures**, and **chutes** that convert kinetic energy into potential energy in controlled steps.

### 1.2.3 Principle 3 — Vegetative Stabilization
Establish permanent vegetation on sidewalls, headcuts, and surrounding catchment to bind soil with roots, reduce flow velocity, and trap sediment.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Frequently Tested Principle:** "**Control at top → control in middle → control at bottom**" corresponds to *source control → in-channel control → outlet stabilization*.

## 1.3 VEGETATIVE MEASURES FOR GULLY CONTROL

Vegetative measures are the **most economical and ecologically sustainable** methods of gully control, applicable especially in **small and medium gullies**.

### 1.3.1 Functions of Vegetation
- **Root Binding** — Roots create a sub-surface "mesh" that resists detachment
- **Shoot Obstruction** — Stems reduce flow velocity, lowering tractive force
- **Moisture Regulation** — Transpiration reduces soil moisture, increases cohesion
- **Sediment Filtering** — Stems act as strainers, depositing suspended load

### 1.3.2 Recommended Vegetation Types
| Vegetation Type | Application Zone | Key Species |
|---|---|---|
| **Grasses** | Gully bed, banks | Vetiver, Bermuda, Napier |
| **Shrubs** | Gully sidewalls | Lantana, Ipomoea, Bamboo |
| **Trees** | Gully periphery | *Leucaena*, *Casuarina*, Neem |
| **Agroforestry** | Watershed | Teak, Sissoo, Subabul |

### 1.3.3 Limitations
- Ineffective during **establishment period** (2–3 years)
- Cannot withstand **high-velocity flows** (> 3 m/s) in deep gullies
- Requires **fencing/protection** from grazing

## 1.4 TEMPORARY STRUCTURES FOR GULLY CONTROL

Temporary structures are **low-cost, short-life-span** (typically 2–5 years) structures used to:
1. Reduce gully flow velocity
2. Trap sediment
3. Allow vegetation to establish

### 1.4.1 Common Temporary Structures

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Brushwood Check Dam** — Made of locally available branches/sticks woven together and anchored with stakes. Used in **small gullies** with depths < 1.5 m.

**Wattle Fence / Wicker Dam** — Woven bamboo or cane mats fixed across the gully.

**Log Check Dam** — Horizontal logs laid across the gully and anchored to sidewalls.

**Gabion Check Dam** — Stone-filled wire mesh boxes. While semi-permanent, often used as a **transitional structure** before permanent works.

**Loose Rock Check Dam** — Stones piled across the gully without binding.

### 1.4.2 Design of a Brushwood Check Dam
The freeboard and spillway section follow empirical rules:

$$H = \frac{D}{2}$$

where **H** = check dam height (m), **D** = gully depth at the location (m).

The **spillway width** is given by:

$$L = \frac{Q}{C \cdot H^{1.5}}$$

where **Q** = design discharge (m³/s), **C** = weir coefficient ≈ **1.7 m^0.5/s** for broad-crested weirs.

## 1.5 DIVERSION DRAINS

### 1.5.1 Definition and Purpose
A **diversion drain** is an artificially constructed channel that intercepts and diverts runoff away from a vulnerable area (e.g., gully head, unstable slope) to a safe disposal point (grassed waterway, stream, or stabilized outlet).

### 1.5.2 Design Criteria
| Parameter | Recommended Value |
|---|---|
| Longitudinal grade | 0.1% to 0.5% |
| Side slope (earth) | 1.5:1 to 2:1 |
| Side slope (rock) | 1:1 |
| Freeboard | 0.15 m minimum |
| Shape | Trapezoidal preferred |

### 1.5.3 Capacity Equation (Manning's Equation)
The design discharge capacity is given by:

$$Q = \frac{1}{n} \cdot A \cdot R^{2/3} \cdot S^{1/2}$$

where:
- **Q** = discharge (m³/s)
- **n** = Manning's roughness coefficient (0.025 for earth, 0.015 for concrete)
- **A** = cross-sectional area of flow (m²)
- **R** = hydraulic radius (m) = A/P
- **P** = wetted perimeter (m)
- **S** = longitudinal slope (m/m)

## 1.6 GRASSED WATERWAYS

### 1.6.1 Definition
A **grassed waterway** is a natural or constructed channel, shaped to required dimensions, and stabilized with a grass cover, used to convey runoff without causing soil erosion.

### 1.6.2 Intuitive Analogy — The "Velvet Conveyor"
Think of a grassed waterway as a **soft, velvety conveyor belt** for water. The grass blades act like millions of tiny fingers that grab the water and slow it down, while the dense root system below acts as a "Velcro" that holds the conveyor belt in place so it doesn't wash away.

### 1.6.3 Design of Grassed Waterways

**Step 1: Determine Design Discharge** using the Rational Method:

$$Q = \frac{C \cdot I \cdot A}{360}$$

where:
- **Q** = peak discharge (m³/s)
- **C** = runoff coefficient (0.3–0.6 for agricultural watersheds)
- **I** = rainfall intensity (mm/hr) for the chosen return period
- **A** = drainage area (hectares)

**Step 2: Allowable Velocity Criterion**
The design must satisfy:

$$V_{design} \leq V_{allowable}$$

Typical allowable velocities (USDA-SCS) for grassed waterways:

| Cover Type | V_allowable (m/s) |
|---|---|
| Bermuda grass | 1.8–2.4 |
| Kentucky bluegrass | 1.2–1.8 |
| Sod-forming grass | 1.5–2.1 |
| Poor cover | 0.9–1.2 |

**Step 3: Trapezoidal Cross-Section Design**
For a trapezoidal channel with bottom width **b**, depth **y**, and side slope **z:1**:

$$A = b \cdot y + z \cdot y^2$$

$$P = b + 2y\sqrt{1+z^2}$$

$$R = \frac{A}{P}$$

Combining with Manning's equation:

$$Q = \frac{1}{n} \cdot (b \cdot y + z \cdot y^2) \cdot R^{2/3} \cdot S^{1/2}$$

Solve iteratively for **y** given **Q**, **b**, **S**, **n**, **z**.

### 1.6.4 Tractive Force Method (Advanced Design)
A more rigorous design uses the **tractive force (boundary shear stress)** approach:

$$\tau_0 = \gamma \cdot R \cdot S$$

where:
- **τ₀** = boundary shear stress (N/m²)
- **γ** = unit weight of water = **9810 N/m³**
- **R** = hydraulic radius (m)
- **S** = channel slope (m/m)

The permissible tractive stress for grass-lined channels (from USDA-TP-61):

| Cover Condition | Permissible τ (N/m²) |
|---|---|
| Excellent stand, well-maintained | 100–180 |
| Good stand | 60–100 |
| Fair stand | 20–60 |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** Always use the **lower of** (1) allowable velocity method and (2) tractive force method for safer design.

## 1.7 USE OF GEOTEXTILES IN SOIL AND WATER CONSERVATION

### 1.7.1 What Are Geotextiles?
**Geotextiles** are permeable, synthetic fabrics (typically made of polypropylene, polyester, or polyethylene) used in contact with soil/rock for separation, filtration, reinforcement, drainage, or protection.

### 1.7.2 Functions in Gully Control

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Five Primary Functions of Geotextiles (as per KTU syllabus):**
> 1. **Separation** — Prevent mixing of two dissimilar soils
> 2. **Filtration** — Allow water passage while retaining soil particles
> 3. **Reinforcement** — Provide tensile strength to weak soil
> 4. **Drainage** — Convey water within the plane of the fabric
> 5. **Protection** — Absorb stress and prevent surface damage

### 1.7.3 Applications in Gully/Ravine Reclamation
- **Lining of grassed waterways** on steep slopes
- **Wrapping of gabion check dams**
- **Underlay below riprap** in high-velocity channels
- **Slope stabilization** on gully sidewalls
- **Filter layers** behind retaining structures

### 1.7.4 Design Considerations
| Property | Typical Range |
|---|---|
| Mass per unit area | 100–400 g/m² |
| Tensile strength | 8–50 kN/m |
| Apparent opening size (AOS) | 0.07–0.30 mm |
| Permeability | > 10 × soil permeability |

**Survivability Criteria:**

$$T_{required} = T_{ult} \cdot F_s$$

where **F_s** = factor of safety (typically **3.0–5.0** for installation damage, creep, chemical degradation, and biological degradation combined).

## 1.8 PERMANENT STRUCTURES — OVERVIEW

Permanent structures are used when:
- The gully is **large** (> 5 m deep)
- **Vegetative/temporary measures** are insufficient
- The structure must provide **long-term stability** (life > 25 years)

### 1.8.1 Classification of Permanent Structures

> [!NOTE]
> **Major Permanent Structures for Gully Control (as per KTU Module 3):**
> - **Check Dams** (Drop, Chute, Pipe-drop)
> - **Drop Spillways** (Straight drop, Box-inlet drop)
> - **Chute Spillways**
> - **Drop Inlet Spillways**
> - **Pipe Spillways**

## 1.9 CHECK DAMS

### 1.9.1 Definition
A **check dam** is a small dam constructed across a gully or channel to reduce flow velocity, trap sediment, and stabilize the channel bed.

### 1.9.2 Types of Check Dams
| Type | Material | Typical Use |
|---|---|---|
| **Loose-stone check dam** | Stones, no binding | Small gullies, temporary |
| **Gabion check dam** | Stones in wire mesh | Semi-permanent, flexible |
| **Masonry/concrete check dam** | Mortared stone or concrete | Permanent, large gullies |
| **Brushwood check dam** | Twigs/branches | Temporary, small gullies |

### 1.9.3 Design Requirements of a Check Dam
1. **Spillway capacity** must carry design discharge
2. **Height** limited to **2.0 m** for stability
3. **Sidewall embedment** minimum **0.5 m** into gully sides
4. **Apron** for energy dissipation downstream
5. **Trash rack** to prevent debris blockage (for pipe drop inlets)

### 1.9.4 Spillway Design (Broad-Crested Weir)
For a check dam with a rectangular spillway of width **L** and design head **H**:

$$Q = C \cdot L \cdot H^{1.5}$$

where **C** = **1.45 m^0.5/s** for a sharp-crested weir, **1.7 m^0.5/s** for broad-crested weir.
