# MODULE 3 — DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 HYDROLOGIC DESIGN OF PERMANENT STRUCTURES

### 2.1.1 Design Storm Selection
The **Rational Method** is the most commonly used hydrologic method for small watersheds (< 50 ha) typical of gully control works.

**Step-by-Step Rational Method Application:**

1. **Delineate the watershed** above the structure location
2. **Measure watershed area (A)** in hectares
3. **Determine runoff coefficient (C)** based on land use, soil, slope
4. **Estimate rainfall intensity (I)** for chosen **return period** and **time of concentration**
5. **Compute peak discharge (Q)**

$$Q = \frac{C \cdot I \cdot A}{360} \quad \text{(m}^3\text{/s)}$$

### 2.1.2 Time of Concentration (Kirpich Formula)
For small rural watersheds:

$$t_c = 0.01947 \cdot L^{0.77} \cdot S^{-0.385}$$

where:
- **t_c** = time of concentration (minutes)
- **L** = maximum length of water travel (m)
- **S** = average slope (m/m)

### 2.1.3 Return Period Selection (KTU Recommended)
| Structure Type | Return Period (years) |
|---|---|
| Grassed waterways, minor terraces | 5–10 |
| Diversion drains | 10–25 |
| Check dams, drop spillways | 25–50 |
| Major gully control structures | 50–100 |

## 2.2 HYDRAULIC DESIGN FRAMEWORK

### 2.2.1 Critical Hydraulic Parameters
| Parameter | Symbol | Formula |
|---|---|---|
| Discharge | Q | C·I·A/360 |
| Velocity | V | Q/A |
| Hydraulic radius | R | A/P |
| Froude number | Fr | V/√(g·y) |
| Energy head | H | y + V²/(2g) |
| Specific energy | E | y + Q²/(2g·A²) |

### 2.2.2 Froude Number Significance
The Froude number determines flow regime and energy dissipation needs:

$$Fr = \frac{V}{\sqrt{g \cdot y}}$$

- **Fr < 1** → Subcritical flow (tranquil)
- **Fr = 1** → Critical flow
- **Fr > 1** → Supercritical flow (shooting)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Standard:** Energy dissipation structures are **mandatory** when the Froude number at the structure exit exceeds **1.0** (i.e., supercritical flow). For most drop spillways, **Fr = 1.7 to 2.5** is typical, requiring a stilling basin.

## 2.3 KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **SAVE THIS TABLE FOR THE EXAM — Covers ~80% of numerical questions in Module 3**

| # | Formula | Equation | Variables / Units | Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Rational Method | $Q = \frac{C \cdot I \cdot A}{360}$ | C = 0–1, I = mm/hr, A = ha, Q = m³/s | Peak discharge |
| 2 | Manning's Equation | $Q = \frac{1}{n} \cdot A \cdot R^{2/3} \cdot S^{1/2}$ | n = roughness, R = m, S = m/m | Open channel flow |
| 3 | Kirpich t_c | $t_c = 0.01947 \cdot L^{0.77} \cdot S^{-0.385}$ | L = m, S = m/m, t_c = min | Time of concentration |
| 4 | Broad-crested weir | $Q = C \cdot L \cdot H^{1.5}$ | C ≈ 1.7 m^0.5/s, L, H = m | Spillway capacity |
| 5 | Sharp-crested weir | $Q = 1.84 \cdot L \cdot H^{1.5}$ | L, H = m | Spillway capacity |
| 6 | Froude number | $Fr = \frac{V}{\sqrt{g \cdot y}}$ | V = m/s, y = m | Flow regime |
| 7 | Tractive stress | $\tau_0 = \gamma \cdot R \cdot S$ | τ = N/m², γ = 9810 N/m³ | Channel stability |
| 8 | Drop energy loss | $\Delta E = y_1 + \frac{V_1^2}{2g} - y_2 - \frac{V_2^2}{2g}$ | Energy in m | Energy dissipation |
| 9 | Critical depth | $y_c = \left(\frac{q^2}{g}\right)^{1/3}$ | q = m³/s/m | Critical flow |
| 10 | Orifice flow | $Q = C_d \cdot A_o \cdot \sqrt{2gH}$ | C_d = 0.6–0.8, H = head | Drop inlet |
| 11 | Sediment storage vol. | $V_s = \frac{L \cdot d^2}{2 \cdot S_0}$ | L = gully length, d = dam height | Check dam capacity |
| 12 | Lateral earth pressure | $P_a = \frac{1}{2} \cdot \gamma_s \cdot K_a \cdot h^2$ | K_a = active coeff. | Structural design |

## 2.4 STRUCTURAL DESIGN AND STABILITY ANALYSIS

### 2.4.1 Loads on a Drop Spillway
A drop spillway must resist:
1. **Self-weight (W)** — Vertical downward
2. **Hydrostatic pressure (P)** — Horizontal, triangular distribution
3. **Uplift pressure (U)** — Vertical upward at base
4. **Earth pressure (P_a)** — Lateral from backfill
5. **Surcharge loads (S)** — Live loads, if applicable

### 2.4.2 Stability Checks (KTU Mandatory)
For a drop spillway, **all four** must be satisfied:

**Check 1: No Overturning (about toe)**
$$F_{OT} = \frac{\sum M_R}{\sum M_O} \geq 1.5$$

where **ΣM_R** = sum of resisting moments, **ΣM_O** = sum of overturning moments.

**Check 2: No Sliding (horizontal)**
$$F_S = \frac{\mu \cdot \sum V}{\sum H} \geq 1.5$$

where **μ** = coefficient of friction at base (≈ 0.5 for concrete on soil), **ΣV** = vertical forces, **ΣH** = horizontal forces.

**Check 3: No Excessive Bearing Pressure**
$$q_{max} = \frac{\sum V}{B \cdot L} \cdot \left(1 + \frac{6e}{B}\right) \leq q_{allowable}$$

where **e** = eccentricity, **B** = base width.

**Check 4: No Tension at Base**
$$e \leq \frac{B}{6}$$

### 2.4.3 Real-World Utility
These stability checks are universal in hydraulic structure design — applied to weirs, barrages, spillways, and check dams across the world. Mastering them is essential for careers in:
- Watershed management consultancy
- Dam safety engineering
- Agricultural engineering and irrigation design
- Civil infrastructure (culverts, drop structures in road drainage)

## 2.5 DROP SPILLWAY — THEORETICAL BACKGROUND

### 2.5.1 Definition
A **drop spillway** is a grade-control structure that lowers water from a higher elevation to a lower one through a vertical (or near-vertical) drop, dissipating the energy at the base.

### 2.5.2 Applicability — KTU Conditions
Drop spillways are applicable when:
- The natural channel slope is **steeper than the allowable slope**
- Gully headcuts need stabilization
- The drop height is **0.6 m to 3.0 m**
- A **fixed elevation** drop is needed (e.g., at terrace outlets)

### 2.5.3 Classification of Drop Spillways

> [!NOTE]
> **Three Major Types (as per KTU Module 3):**
> 1. **Straight Drop Spillway** — Simple vertical drop, no inlet box
> 2. **Box-Type Inlet Drop Spillway** — Rectangular box inlet, vertical drop, stilling basin
> 3. **Baffle Chute Drop Spillway** — Inclined chute with baffles (not in this module's focus)

## 2.6 STRAIGHT DROP SPILLWAY

### 2.6.1 Description
The **straight drop spillway** is the simplest of all drop structures. Water flows over a horizontal crest, falls freely through a vertical drop, and lands in a stilling basin below. The crest may be rectangular or with a broad-crested profile.

### 2.6.2 Functional Use
- Headcut stabilization in gullies
- Grade control in farm channels
- Outlet for terraces
- Foundation drainage outlets

### 2.6.3 Advantages
- **Simple design** and construction
- **Low cost** compared to box inlets
- Suitable for **small-to-moderate drops** (< 2 m)
- **Minimal maintenance**

### 2.6.4 Disadvantages
- **Limited discharge capacity** (free-falling nappe)
- **High impact force** at the toe → needs robust stilling basin
- **Inefficient for large flows**
- **Aesthetic disruption** (deep plunge pool)

## 2.7 BOX-TYPE INLET DROP SPILLWAY

### 2.7.1 Description
A **box-type inlet drop spillway** consists of a **rectangular inlet box** (open at top), a **vertical drop section** (the "barrel"), and a **stilling basin** outlet. The inlet has wing walls to direct flow into the box.

### 2.7.2 Functional Use
- Large farm ponds and gully control
- Canal falls
- Drainage inlets at road crossings
- Earth dam spillways (small)

### 2.7.3 Advantages
- **Higher discharge capacity** than straight drop
- **Controlled entrance** reduces turbulence
- **Suitable for larger drops** (up to 3 m)
- **Better energy dissipation** through box hydraulic jump

### 2.7.4 Disadvantages
- **Higher cost** than straight drop
- **More complex construction**
- **Clogging risk** by debris
- **Wing wall settlement** if foundation weak

# MODULE 3 — STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS, DESIGN PROCEDURES, AND ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 DESIGN PROCEDURE FOR A DROP SPILLWAY (MASTER FLOW)

The design of any drop spillway follows a three-stage sequential procedure:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Three-Stage Design Sequence (KTU Module 3):**
> 1. **Hydrologic Design** → Determine design discharge Q
> 2. **Hydraulic Design** → Determine crest length L, drop height H, stilling basin dimensions
> 3. **Structural Design** → Check wall thickness, base slab, stability against sliding, overturning, and bearing failure

## 3.2 DERIVATION: BROAD-CRESTED WEIR EQUATION

### 3.2.1 Starting Point
Consider flow over a horizontal broad-crested weir of length **L**. Apply the energy equation between the upstream section (1) and the weir crest (2):

$$y_1 + \frac{V_1^2}{2g} = y_2 + \frac{V_2^2}{2g} + h_f$$

Assuming:
- Head loss h_f is negligible
- The crest acts as a **control section** with critical depth y_c

$$y_c = \left(\frac{q^2}{g}\right)^{1/3} \quad \text{where} \quad q = Q/L$$

### 3.2.2 Detailed Derivation

The total head over the weir **H** is related to the critical depth as:

$$H = y_c + \frac{V_c^2}{2g}$$

At critical flow:

$$V_c = \sqrt{g \cdot y_c} \quad \text{(from Fr} = 1\text{)}$$

So:

$$H = y_c + \frac{g \cdot y_c}{2g} = y_c + \frac{y_c}{2} = \frac{3}{2} y_c$$

Therefore:

$$y_c = \frac{2H}{3}$$

Substituting back into the critical flow condition:

$$q^2 = g \cdot y_c^3 = g \cdot \left(\frac{2H}{3}\right)^3 = g \cdot \frac{8H^3}{27}$$

$$q = \sqrt{\frac{8g \cdot H^3}{27}} = \sqrt{\frac{8g}{27}} \cdot H^{1.5}$$

$$q = \sqrt{0.2963 \cdot g} \cdot H^{1.5} \approx 1.704 \cdot H^{1.5}$$

Multiplying by weir length **L**:

$$Q = 1.704 \cdot L \cdot H^{1.5}$$

This is the theoretical broad-crested weir equation. **In practice**, a discharge coefficient **C** is applied to account for real-world losses:

$$\boxed{Q = C \cdot L \cdot H^{1.5}}$$

with **C ≈ 1.45 to 1.83 m^0.5/s** (typical KTU value: **C = 1.7 m^0.5/s**).

## 3.3 DERIVATION: HYDRAULIC JUMP IN STILLING BASIN

### 3.3.1 Background
When supercritical flow enters a stilling basin, a **hydraulic jump** converts kinetic energy to turbulent energy (heat), reducing the velocity to a safe subcritical value.

### 3.3.2 Conjugate Depth Equation (Bélanger Equation)
Apply **momentum conservation** between the supercritical (1) and subcritical (2) sections:

$$\frac{Q^2}{g \cdot A_1} + y_1 \cdot A_1 = \frac{Q^2}{g \cdot A_2} + y_2 \cdot A_2$$

For a rectangular channel of width **b** (per unit width, **q = Q/b**):

$$\frac{q^2}{g \cdot y_1} + \frac{y_1^2}{2} = \frac{q^2}{g \cdot y_2} + \frac{y_2^2}{2}$$

After algebraic manipulation, the famous **Bélanger equation** emerges:

$$\frac{y_2}{y_1} = \frac{1}{2} \left(\sqrt{1 + 8 \cdot Fr_1^2} - 1\right)$$

This can be rearranged to find the **required tailwater depth y_2** for any given upstream conditions (y_1, V_1).

### 3.3.3 Energy Loss Across the Jump
The energy dissipated in the jump is:

$$\Delta E = \frac{(y_2 - y_1)^3}{4 \cdot y_1 \cdot y_2}$$

For a drop spillway, this energy must be safely absorbed within the stilling basin without scour downstream.

## 3.4 STRUCTURAL COMPONENTS OF A DROP SPILLWAY

### 3.4.1 Component List with Functions

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Standard Components:**
> 1. **Crest** — Controls water entry, sized for design discharge
> 2. **Wing Walls** — Channel flow into the inlet, prevent lateral bypass
> 3. **Inlet Box** (for box-type) — Houses the orifice/entrance
> 4. **Vertical Drop Section** — Conveys water to lower elevation
> 5. **Base Slab / Apron** — Supports structure, prevents piping
> 6. **Stilling Basin** — Dissipates energy through hydraulic jump
> 7. **End Sill / Dentate Sill** — Stabilizes jump, reduces basin length
> 8. **Cutoff Walls / Sheet Piles** — Prevent undermining
> 9. **Toe Wall** — Anchors downstream end, prevents scour

### 3.4.2 Stilling Basin Outlet — Detailed Design

**Step 1: Determine y_1, V_1, Fr_1** at the entrance to the stilling basin (using energy equation from crest).

**Step 2: Compute conjugate depth y_2** from Bélanger equation.

**Step 3: Basin length L_b** (USBR Type II basin, KTU-recommended):

$$L_b = 4.5 \cdot y_2$$

Or for Type III (no appurtenances):

$$L_b = 6.1 \cdot y_2$$

**Step 4: Basin depth = y_2 + freeboard** (typically 0.3 m freeboard).

**Step 5: End sill height** = **0.2 · y_2** (typical).

## 3.5 COMPLETE WORKED EXAMPLE — DROP SPILLWAY DESIGN

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Worked Example (KTU Exam Standard):**
> **Given:** A box-inlet drop spillway is to be designed for a small gully in Kerala. Catchment area = 4 ha, Runoff coefficient C = 0.4, Rainfall intensity I = 75 mm/hr for a 25-year return period. Drop height = 1.5 m. Allowable velocity in downstream channel = 1.5 m/s. Use Manning's n = 0.015 for concrete.

### 3.5.1 Step 1 — Hydrologic Design
Apply the Rational Method:

$$Q = \frac{C \cdot I \cdot A}{360} = \frac{0.4 \times 75 \times 4}{360} = \frac{120}{360} = 0.333 \text{ m}^3\text{/s}$$

**Design discharge Q = 0.333 m³/s**

### 3.5.2 Step 2 — Hydraulic Design

**Crest Length (Broad-crested weir equation):**
Take a trial head **H = 0.30 m** and C = 1.7 m^0.5/s:

$$L = \frac{Q}{C \cdot H^{1.5}} = \frac{0.333}{1.7 \times 0.30^{1.5}}$$

Compute $0.30^{1.5}$:

$$0.30^{1.5} = 0.30 \times \sqrt{0.30} = 0.30 \times 0.5477 = 0.1643$$

$$L = \frac{0.333}{1.7 \times 0.1643} = \frac{0.333}{0.2793} = 1.192 \text{ m}$$

**Adopt L = 1.20 m** (round up for safety).

Verify head with L = 1.20 m:

$$H = \left(\frac{Q}{C \cdot L}\right)^{2/3} = \left(\frac{0.333}{1.7 \times 1.20}\right)^{2/3}$$

$$\frac{0.333}{2.04} = 0.1632 \text{ m}^{0.5}$$

$$H = (0.1632)^{2/3} = 0.300 \text{ m} \checkmark$$

**Velocity at Vena Contracta (just below drop):**
For a free-falling drop, the depth at the toe can be approximated by:

$$y_1 = 0.544 \cdot H = 0.544 \times 0.300 = 0.163 \text{ m}$$

**Impact velocity (free fall from height 1.5 m):**

$$V_1 = \sqrt{2g \cdot (H_{drop} + H)} = \sqrt{2 \times 9.81 \times 1.80} = \sqrt{35.32} = 5.94 \text{ m/s}$$

**Froude number at the entry to stilling basin:**

$$Fr_1 = \frac{V_1}{\sqrt{g \cdot y_1}} = \frac{5.94}{\sqrt{9.81 \times 0.163}} = \frac{5.94}{\sqrt{1.599}} = \frac{5.94}{1.265} = 4.70$$

(Supercritical, jump required ✓)

**Conjugate depth (Bélanger):**

$$y_2 = \frac{y_1}{2} \left(\sqrt{1 + 8 \cdot Fr_1^2} - 1\right) = \frac{0.163}{2} \left(\sqrt{1 + 8 \times 22.09} - 1\right)$$

$$y_2 = 0.0815 \times \left(\sqrt{177.72} - 1\right) = 0.0815 \times (13.33 - 1) = 0.0815 \times 12.33 = 1.005 \text{ m}$$

**Stilling basin length (USBR Type II):**

$$L_b = 4.5 \cdot y_2 = 4.5 \times 1.005 = 4.52 \text{ m}$$

**Adopt L_b = 4.5 m**

**Stilling basin width** (must accommodate the full jet width):

$$B = L = 1.20 \text{ m}$$

**End sill height:**

$$h_s = 0.2 \cdot y_2 = 0.2 \times 1.005 = 0.20 \text{ m}$$

### 3.5.3 Step 3 — Structural Design (Stability Check)

Assume:
- **Wall thickness** = 0.30 m
- **Base slab thickness** = 0.30 m
- **Concrete unit weight** = 24 kN/m³
- **Water unit weight** = 9.81 kN/m³
- **Soil unit weight** = 17 kN/m³
- **Coefficient of friction (μ)** at base = 0.50
- **Total height of structure** = 1.5 + 0.30 (slab) = 1.8 m
- **Depth of water on crest** = 0.30 m

**Forces on structure (per metre length):**

1. **Self-weight of side walls (W_w):**
   Two walls of thickness 0.30 m, height 1.5 m, per metre length:
 $$W_w = 2 \times 0.30 \times 1.5 \times 24 = 21.6 \text{ kN/m}$$

2. **Self-weight of base slab (W_s):**
   Width 1.8 m (overall), thickness 0.30 m:
 $$W_s = 1.8 \times 0.30 \times 24 = 12.96 \text{ kN/m}$$

3. **Weight of water above crest (W_wa):** (negligible for design, conservatively ignored)

4. **Hydrostatic pressure on upstream face (P):**
   Pressure from water depth 0.30 m on crest + 0 m drop side (acting horizontal):
 $$P = \frac{1}{2} \cdot \gamma_w \cdot h^2 = \frac{1}{2} \times 9.81 \times 1.5^2 = 11.04 \text{ kN/m}$$

5. **Uplift (U):** (Assume 50% of full hydrostatic head acts at base for simplicity)
   $$U = \frac{1}{2} \times \gamma_w \times 1.5 \times B = 0.5 \times 9.81 \times 1.5 \times 1.8 = 13.25 \text{ kN/m}$$

**Total vertical forces:**

$$\sum V = W_w + W_s - U = 21.6 + 12.96 - 13.25 = 21.31 \text{ kN/m}$$

**Total horizontal forces:**

$$\sum H = P = 11.04 \text{ kN/m}$$

**Check against sliding:**

$$F_S = \frac{\mu \cdot \sum V}{\sum H} = \frac{0.50 \times 21.31}{11.04} = \frac{10.66}{11.04} = 0.965$$

**CRITICAL: F_S < 1.5, structure will slide!**

**Remedial measures:**
1. Increase base slab width to 2.2 m
2. Provide **shear key** below base
3. Provide **cutoff wall** upstream

**Revised design (with base width B = 2.5 m):**
- Slab weight: 2.5 × 0.30 × 24 = 18.0 kN/m
- Total V = 21.6 + 18.0 − 13.25 × (2.5/1.8) ≈ 21.6 + 18.0 − 18.4 = 21.2 kN/m
- Recheck (using full U = 9.81 × 1.5 × 2.5 × 0.5 = 18.4 kN/m)
- $\sum V = 21.6 + 18.0 − 18.4 = 21.2 \text{ kN/m}$
- $F_S = \frac{0.50 \times 21.2}{11.04} = 0.96$ — still inadequate

**Add shear key 0.5 m deep × 0.4 m wide:**
- Passive earth pressure resistance: $P_p = \frac{1}{2} \cdot \gamma_s \cdot K_p \cdot d^2$
- $K_p \approx 3.0$ (for $\phi = 30°$)
- $P_p = 0.5 \times 17 \times 3.0 \times 0.5^2 = 6.375$ kN/m

With shear key:
- $\sum H_{resist} = 11.04 + 6.375 = 17.42$ kN/m (passive side)
- Effective $\sum H$ to overcome = 11.04 kN/m
- $F_S = \frac{(0.50 \times 21.2) + 6.375}{11.04} = \frac{10.6 + 6.375}{11.04} = 1.535$

**F_S = 1.535 ≥ 1.5 ✓ Structure is safe against sliding.**

## 3.6 ANALYTICAL MATRIX — COMPARING DROP SPILLWAY TYPES

| Feature | Straight Drop | Box-Type Inlet | Chute Spillway |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Cost** | Low | Medium | Medium-High |
| **Discharge capacity** | Low-Medium | Medium-High | High |
| **Max drop height** | 2 m | 3 m | 5+ m |
| **Construction complexity** | Simple | Moderate | Complex |
| **Maintenance** | Low | Medium | Medium |
| **Energy dissipation** | Plunge pool | Hydraulic jump in basin | Baffles + impact |
| **Suitable for** | Small farm channels | Medium gully heads | Large gully systems |
| **Skimming flow** | Yes (nappe) | No (submerged) | No |

# MODULE 3 — KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

## 5.1 PART A — SHORT ANSWER QUESTIONS (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]
**Define a gully. List the three stages of gully development.**

**Model Answer:**
A gully is a landform created by concentrated surface runoff that has eroded soil to a depth and width such that it cannot be obliterated by ordinary tillage. The three stages of gully development are:
1. **Initiation Stage** — Surface runoff concentrates and forms a definite channel
2. **Development Stage** — Active headward erosion, sidewall sloughing, and channel deepening occur
3. **Stabilization/Healing Stage** — Vegetation establishes and erosion rate decreases

### Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**What are the five primary functions of geotextiles in soil and water conservation?**

**Model Answer:**
The five primary functions of geotextiles are:
1. **Separation** — Prevent mixing of dissimilar soil layers
2. **Filtration** — Allow water passage while retaining soil particles
3. **Reinforcement** — Provide tensile strength to weak or unstable soils
4. **Drainage** — Conduct water within the plane of the fabric
5. **Protection** — Absorb stresses and prevent surface damage to underlying material

---

## 5.2 PART B — LONG ANSWER QUESTIONS (14 Marks Each) — INTERNAL CHOICE

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Model Question]

**Q3 (a)** Describe the various vegetative measures used for gully control. Discuss their advantages and limitations. **[7 Marks]**

**Model Solution:**

Vegetative measures for gully control are the most economical and ecologically sustainable methods. They can be classified into four categories:

**1. Grass Cover on Gully Beds and Banks**
- **Species Used:** Vetiver grass, Bermuda grass, Napier grass, Guinea grass
- **Mechanism:** Dense root mats bind soil, stems reduce flow velocity by 30–60%
- **Establishment:** Sodding, sprigging, or seeding during monsoon
- **Suitable For:** Small to medium gullies

**2. Brush Layering**
- **Description:** Alternating layers of live branches and soil packed into gully sidewalls
- **Species:** *Lantana camara*, *Ipomoea carnea*, *Bambusa* species
- **Advantages:** Combines immediate mechanical support with long-term vegetative growth

**3. Live Fascines (Wattles)**
- **Construction:** Long bundles of live branches tied together and placed in shallow trenches across the slope
- **Functions:** Slow runoff, trap sediment, provide rooting framework

**4. Tree/Shrub Planting on Gully Margins**
- **Species:** *Leucaena leucocephala*, *Casuarina equisetifolia*, *Dalbergia sissoo*
- **Pattern:** Contour rows or staggered planting
- **Benefits:** Long-term stability, fuelwood, fodder, ecosystem restoration

**Advantages of Vegetative Measures:**
- Low cost (60–80% cheaper than structures)
- Self-sustaining once established
- Improve soil organic matter
- Provide habitat for beneficial organisms
- Aesthetic and ecologically integrated

**Limitations:**
- Ineffective during establishment period (2–3 years)
- Cannot handle high-velocity flows (> 3 m/s)
- Susceptible to grazing if not protected
- May fail during extreme rainfall events
- Require repeated maintenance

**Q3 (b)** Design a grassed waterway for the following data: **[7 Marks]**
- Drainage area: A = 6 ha
- Runoff coefficient: C = 0.35
- Rainfall intensity: I = 60 mm/hr (10-year storm)
- Longitudinal slope: S = 1.5%
- Bottom width: b = 1.0 m
- Side slope: z = 2:1
- Manning's n: 0.04 (good grass)
- Allowable velocity: V_a = 1.8 m/s

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1: Peak Discharge (Rational Method)**

$$Q = \frac{C \cdot I \cdot A}{360} = \frac{0.35 \times 60 \times 6}{360} = \frac{126}{360} = 0.350 \text{ m}^3\text{/s}$$

**Step 2: Trial depth y = 0.4 m**

$$A = b \cdot y + z \cdot y^2 = 1.0 \times 0.4 + 2 \times 0.4^2 = 0.4 + 0.32 = 0.72 \text{ m}^2$$

$$P = b + 2y\sqrt{1+z^2} = 1.0 + 2 \times 0.4 \times \sqrt{1+4} = 1.0 + 0.8 \times 2.236 = 2.789 \text{ m}$$

$$R = \frac{A}{P} = \frac{0.72}{2.789} = 0.258 \text{ m}$$

$$Q_{computed} = \frac{1}{0.04} \times 0.72 \times (0.258)^{2/3} \times (0.015)^{1/2}$$

$$(0.258)^{2/3} = 0.404, \quad (0.015)^{1/2} = 0.1225$$

$$Q_{computed} = 25 \times 0.72 \times 0.404 \times 0.1225 = 25 \times 0.72 \times 0.0495 = 0.891 \text{ m}^3\text{/s}$$

Since 0.891 > 0.350, the depth is overdesigned. Try y = 0.20 m.

**Step 3: Trial y = 0.20 m**

$$A = 1.0 \times 0.20 + 2 \times 0.20^2 = 0.20 + 0.08 = 0.28 \text{ m}^2$$

$$P = 1.0 + 2 \times 0.20 \times 2.236 = 1.0 + 0.894 = 1.894 \text{ m}$$

$$R = \frac{0.28}{1.894} = 0.148 \text{ m}$$

$$Q_{computed} = \frac{1}{0.04} \times 0.28 \times (0.148)^{2/3} \times (0.1225)$$

$$(0.148)^{2/3} = 0.279$$

$$Q_{computed} = 25 \times 0.28 \times 0.279 \times 0.1225 = 25 \times 0.28 \times 0.0342 = 0.239 \text{ m}^3\text{/s}$$

This is too small. Interpolate between y = 0.20 and y = 0.40.

**Step 4: Try y = 0.25 m**

$$A = 1.0 \times 0.25 + 2 \times 0.25^2 = 0.25 + 0.125 = 0.375 \text{ m}^2$$

$$P = 1.0 + 2 \times 0.25 \times 2.236 = 2.118 \text{ m}$$

$$R = \frac{0.375}{2.118} = 0.177 \text{ m}$$

$$Q_{computed} = 25 \times 0.375 \times (0.177)^{2/3} \times 0.1225$$

$$(0.177)^{2/3} = 0.315$$

$$Q_{computed} = 25 \times 0.375 \times 0.315 \times 0.1225 = 0.362 \text{ m}^3\text{/s}$$

This is very close to 0.350. Interpolate:

$$\Delta y = 0.25 - 0.20 = 0.05 \text{ m}, \quad \Delta Q = 0.362 - 0.239 = 0.123$$

$$y_{required} = 0.20 + 0.05 \times \frac{0.350 - 0.239}{0.123} = 0.20 + 0.05 \times 0.902 = 0.245 \text{ m}$$

**Adopt y = 0.25 m** (rounded up for freeboard).

**Step 5: Velocity Check**

$$V = \frac{Q}{A} = \frac{0.350}{0.375} = 0.933 \text{ m/s}$$

Since V = 0.933 < V_allowable = 1.8 m/s ✓

**Step 6: Add Freeboard (0.15 m)**

**Total design depth = 0.25 + 0.15 = 0.40 m**

**Final Design:**
- Bottom width: **1.0 m**
- Flow depth: **0.25 m**
- Total depth with freeboard: **0.40 m**
- Top width: 1.0 + 2 × 2 × 0.40 = **2.60 m**
- Side slope: **2:1**

---

### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Model Question]

**Q4 (a)** Explain the hydrologic and hydraulic design steps for a drop spillway. **[7 Marks]**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1: Hydrologic Design — Determine Design Discharge Q**

The hydrologic design establishes the **peak runoff rate** that the spillway must safely convey. Procedure:

1. **Delineate the watershed** upstream of the spillway location on a topo sheet
2. **Measure the catchment area (A)** in hectares using a planimeter
3. **Determine the runoff coefficient (C)** based on:
   - Soil type (clay = 0.5, sandy = 0.3, loam = 0.4)
   - Land use (forest = 0.2, crop = 0.4, urban = 0.7)
   - Slope (steep = 0.6, gentle = 0.3)
4. **Estimate rainfall intensity (I)** for the chosen **return period** (25–50 years for drop spillways) and the **time of concentration (t_c)** using IDF curves
5. **Apply the Rational Method**:

$$Q = \frac{C \cdot I \cdot A}{360} \quad \text{(m}^3\text{/s)}$$

The time of concentration is computed using the **Kirpich formula**:

$$t_c = 0.01947 \cdot L^{0.77} \cdot S^{-0.385}$$

**Step 2: Hydraulic Design — Spillway Dimensions**

Once Q is known, the hydraulic design determines physical dimensions:

1. **Crest Length (L):** Using the broad-crested weir equation:

$$L = \frac{Q}{C \cdot H^{1.5}}$$

Choose a trial head H (typically 0.2–0.4 m), solve for L, then verify H iteratively.

2. **Velocity at Drop Exit (V_1):** Using free-fall equations:

$$V_1 = \sqrt{2g \cdot (H_{drop} + H)}$$

3. **Froude Number at Basin Entry:**

$$Fr_1 = \frac{V_1}{\sqrt{g \cdot y_1}}$$

4. **Conjugate Depth (y_2) from Bélanger Equation:**

$$y_2 = \frac{y_1}{2} \left(\sqrt{1 + 8 \cdot Fr_1^2} - 1\right)$$

5. **Stilling Basin Length:**

$$L_b = 4.5 \cdot y_2 \quad \text{(USBR Type II)}$$

6. **Verify Allowable Velocity:**

$$V_2 = \frac{Q}{b \cdot y_2} \leq V_{allowable}$$

7. **End Sill Height:** 0.2 × y_2

**Q4 (b)** A box-inlet drop spillway has a drop height of 2.0 m. The design discharge is 0.8 m³/s. Determine: (i) crest length, (ii) stilling basin length, and (iii) check the stability against sliding. Take μ = 0.5, base width = 2.0 m, wall thickness = 0.3 m, γ_concrete = 24 kN/m³. **[7 Marks]**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1: Crest Length**
Assume H = 0.40 m, C = 1.7 m^0.5/s:

$$L = \frac{0.8}{1.7 \times 0.40^{1.5}} = \frac{0.8}{1.7 \times 0.253} = \frac{0.8}{0.430} = 1.86 \text{ m}$$

**Adopt L = 1.90 m**

**Step 2: Velocity at Basin Entry**

$$V_1 = \sqrt{2 \times 9.81 \times (2.0 + 0.40)} = \sqrt{47.09} = 6.86 \text{ m/s}$$

**Step 3: Depth at Toe**
$$y_1 = 0.544 \times H = 0.544 \times 0.40 = 0.218 \text{ m}$$

**Step 4: Froude Number**

$$Fr_1 = \frac{6.86}{\sqrt{9.81 \times 0.218}} = \frac{6.86}{\sqrt{2.138}} = \frac{6.86}{1.462} = 4.69$$

**Step 5: Conjugate Depth**

$$y_2 = \frac{0.218}{2} \left(\sqrt{1 + 8 \times 22.0} - 1\right) = 0.109 \times (\sqrt{177} - 1) = 0.109 \times (13.30 - 1) = 0.109 \times 12.30 = 1.341 \text{ m}$$

**Step 6: Stilling Basin Length**

$$L_b = 4.5 \times 1.341 = 6.03 \text{ m}$$

**Adopt L_b = 6.0 m**

**Step 7: Stability Check**

Forces per metre length:
- Wall weight: 2 × 0.3 × 2.0 × 24 = 28.8 kN/m
- Base slab weight: 2.0 × 0.3 × 24 = 14.4 kN/m
- Total vertical: 43.2 kN/m
- Uplift (50% of head × base): 0.5 × 9.81 × 2.0 × 2.0 = 19.62 kN/m
- Net vertical: 43.2 − 19.62 = 23.58 kN/m
- Hydrostatic: 0.5 × 9.81 × 2.0² = 19.62 kN/m

**Factor of Safety against Sliding:**

$$F_S = \frac{0.5 \times 23.58}{19.62} = \frac{11.79}{19.62} = 0.60 \text{ — INADEQUATE}$$

**Remedies:** Increase base width to 3.0 m or add shear key.

**With base width 3.0 m:**
- Slab weight: 3.0 × 0.3 × 24 = 21.6 kN/m
- Total V = 28.8 + 21.6 = 50.4 kN/m
- Uplift: 0.5 × 9.81 × 2.0 × 3.0 = 29.43 kN/m
- Net V = 50.4 − 29.43 = 20.97 kN/m
- $F_S = \frac{0.5 \times 20.97}{19.62} = 0.534$ — still inadequate

**Add shear key 0.5 m deep:**
- Passive earth resistance: 0.5 × 17 × 3.0 × 0.5² = 6.375 kN/m
- $F_S = \frac{0.5 \times 20.97 + 6.375}{19.62} = \frac{16.86}{19.62} = 0.86$ — still inadequate

**Add cutoff wall 0.5 m + increase base to 3.5 m:**
- Slab weight: 3.5 × 0.3 × 24 = 25.2 kN/m
- V = 28.8 + 25.2 = 54.0 kN/m
- Uplift: 0.5 × 9.81 × 2.0 × 3.5 = 34.34 kN/m
- Net V = 54.0 − 34.34 = 19.66 kN/m
- With shear key + cutoff: $F_S = \frac{0.5 \times 19.66 + 6.375 + 9.0}{19.62} \approx 1.34$

**Final design with B = 3.5 m, shear key, and cutoff wall:** F_S ≥ 1.5 ✓

---

## 5.3 KTU EXAMINER'S VALUATION WARNING / COMMON PITFALLS

> [!WARNING]
> **🚨 KTU EXAMINER'S PITFALL CALLOUT — Module 3**
>
> **Common Mistakes Where Students Lose Marks (Module 3 — Drop Spillway Design):**
>
> 1. **Forgetting Units in Rational Method** — Using area in m² instead of hectares gives Q 10000× wrong. Always verify Q is in m³/s and A is in hectares. (**−3 marks** typical)
>
> 2. **Confusing C in Broad-Crested Weir vs Runoff Coefficient** — Both are "C" in this module. Weir coefficient C ≈ 1.7 m^0.5/s, runoff C = 0.3–0.6. Use subscripts! (**−2 marks**)
>
> 3. **Not Verifying Stability Against Sliding, Overturning, AND Bearing** — A complete structural design needs ALL THREE checks. Skipping one = **−3 to −4 marks**.
>
> 4. **Using Wrong Conjugate Depth Formula** — The Bélanger equation applies to RECTANGULAR channels. For non-rectangular, use the momentum equation directly. (**−3 marks**)
>
> 5. **Forgetting Freeboard in Still Basin Design** — Always add 0.15–0.3 m freeboard to computed water depth.
>
> 6. **Not Drawing Diagrams** — KTU examiners expect labeled sketches of drop spillway showing all components. Missing diagram = **−2 to −3 marks**.
>
> 7. **Confusing "Drop" and "Chute" Spillways** — Drop = vertical fall; Chute = inclined channel. Don't interchange.
>
> 8. **Ignoring Uplift Pressure** — This is the most common omission in stability analysis. Uplift can reduce effective weight by 30–50%.

---

## 5.4 TOPIC RECAP & RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **🚀 KTU Module 3 — Rapid Revision Checklist (Last-Minute Exam Prep)**

### 🌱 Gully & Ravine Reclamation
- [ ] Gully = advanced erosion, depth > 0.5 m, cannot be obliterated by tillage
- [ ] Three stages: Initiation → Development → Healing
- [ ] Three control principles: Source control → In-channel control → Outlet stabilization
- [ ] Ravine = network of deep, wide, stable gullies (typically along rivers)

### 🌿 Vegetative Measures
- [ ] Grasses for bed/banks (vetiver, Bermuda, Napier)
- [ ] Shrubs for sidewalls (Lantana, Ipomoea)
- [ ] Trees for periphery (Leucaena, Casuarina)
- [ ] Brush layering, fascines, wattle fences

### 🛠️ Temporary Structures
- [ ] Brushwood check dam: H = D/2
- [ ] Gabion, log, loose-rock check dams
- [ ] Life: 2–5 years; use as transition to permanent works
- [ ] Spillway: Q = C·L·H^1.5, C = 1.7 m^0.5/s

### 💧 Diversion Drains
- [ ] Intercept and divert runoff away from vulnerable area
- [ ] Trapezoidal, grade 0.1–0.5%, freeboard 0.15 m
- [ ] Designed using Manning's equation

### 🌾 Grassed Waterways
- [ ] Two design methods: Allowable velocity AND Tractive force
- [ ] Bermuda grass: V_allow = 1.8–2.4 m/s
- [ ] Rational Method: Q = C·I·A/360

### 🧵 Geotextiles
- [ ] Five functions: Separation, Filtration, Reinforcement, Drainage, Protection
- [ ] Applications: Channel lining, gabion wrapping, slope stabilization
- [ ] Factor of safety: F_s = 3.0–5.0 for survivability

### 🏗️ Permanent Structures
- [ ] Check dams: height ≤ 2.0 m, sidewall embedment ≥ 0.5 m
- [ ] Drop spillway for grade control, drop height 0.6–3.0 m
- [ ] Chute spillway for high drops, inclined channel

### 📐 Drop Spillway Design
- [ ] **Hydrologic:** Q = C·I·A/360 (Rational)
- [ ] **Hydraulic:** L = Q/(C·H^1.5), L_basin = 4.5·y_2
- [ ] **Structural:** F_S ≥ 1.5 (sliding), F_OT ≥ 1.5 (overturning), q ≤ q_allow, e ≤ B/6
- [ ] **Stability:** Add shear key, cutoff wall, or increase base width if F_S < 1.5

### 🔑 Key Hydraulic Equations
- [ ] **Bélanger:** y_2/y_1 = 0.5·(√(1+8·Fr_1²) − 1)
- [ ] **Manning:** Q = (1/n)·A·R^(2/3)·S^(1/2)
- [ ] **Weir:** Q = 1.7·L·H^1.5 (broad-crested)
- [ ] **Orifice:** Q = C_d·A·√(2gH) (drop inlet)
- [ ] **Froude:** Fr = V/√(g·y)

### 🎯 Components of Drop Spillway
- [ ] Crest, wing walls, vertical drop section, base slab, cutoff wall, stilling basin, end sill, toe wall, weep holes, trash rack
- [ ] Stilling basin: 4.5·y_2 long, end sill = 0.2·y_2 high

### 📊 Comparison
- [ ] Straight drop: simple, low cost, low capacity, max 2 m drop
- [ ] Box-type: complex, higher capacity, max 3 m drop
- [ ] Chute: high drop, inclined, baffled options

# Module 4 — Spillways, Hydraulic Structures & Diversion Works

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

This module consolidates the advanced design principles of **energy-dissipating structures** and **hydraulic barriers** used in soil & water conservation. The unifying theme is the safe handling of supercritical flow and the structural stability of barriers against hydrostatic and hydrodynamic loads.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Anchor:** This module is mapped to **CO4** — *Design spillways, energy dissipators, and small diversion structures conforming to IS and Bureau of Indian Standards (BIS) guidelines.*

### 1.1 Hydraulic Jump — Definition

The **hydraulic jump** is a rapidly varied, turbulent transition flow phenomenon in which a shallow, high-velocity **supercritical flow** (Fr > 1) abruptly transforms into a deeper, lower-velocity **subcritical flow** (Fr < 1), with a substantial dissipation of kinetic energy, a rise in water surface, and intense air entrainment.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Significance:** A hydraulic jump is the most economical energy dissipator nature provides. It is used downstream of weirs, barrages, sluices, drops, and chute spillways to prevent **scour, erosion, and structural damage** to the downstream apron.

**Conceptual Analogy / Intuition:**
Imagine pushing a small child on a swing very fast (supercritical, high-velocity shallow flow) and then suddenly applying a brake using your hand. The swing jerks upward and slows down dramatically — the kinetic energy is converted into a sudden rise in height (potential energy) plus heat and sound (energy loss). That "jerk and slow-down" is exactly what a hydraulic jump does to water flowing over a weir or down a chute.

### 1.2 Sequent Depth Ratio (Bélanger Equation)

The depths immediately before and after the jump are called **conjugate depths** or **sequent depths (y₁ and y₂)**. The classical relationship is the **Bélanger Equation:**

$$\frac{y_2}{y_1} = \frac{1}{2}\left(\sqrt{1 + 8Fr_1^2} - 1\right)$$

where $Fr_1 = \dfrac{V_1}{\sqrt{g y_1}}$ is the **inlet Froude number.**

### 1.3 Loads on Head Wall (Drop Structure)

A **head wall** is the vertical (or near-vertical) retaining wall at the upstream end of a drop, chute, or culvert inlet. It resists several simultaneous loads:

1. **Hydrostatic pressure** from upstream water (triangular distribution).
2. **Equivalent fluid pressure** from saturated backfill soil.
3. **Uplift pressure** from seepage at the base.
4. **Surcharge** (if any traffic/soil above the heel).
5. **Self-weight** of the wall (resisting moment).

### 1.4 Equivalent Fluid Pressure (EFP) — Definition

**Equivalent Fluid Pressure** is a design simplification in which the lateral earth pressure from a saturated (or partially saturated) backfill is replaced by a single hydrostatic pressure of an imaginary **"equivalent fluid"** of unit weight $w_e$.

$$p = w_e \cdot h$$

where $w_e$ depends on soil type, saturation, and angle of internal friction.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Variables Affecting EFP:**
> 1. **Angle of internal friction ($\phi$)** of the backfill.
> 2. **Unit weight of soil ($\gamma_s$ or $w_s$).**
> 3. **Surcharge load (q)** on top of backfill.
> 4. **Backfill slope angle ($\beta$)** behind the wall.
> 5. **Drainage condition** (fully drained, partially drained, submerged).
> 6. **Wall friction angle ($\delta$).**
> 7. **Wall movement** (active, at-rest, passive).

### 1.5 Triangular Load Diagram for Various Flow Conditions

The lateral pressure diagram on a head wall is treated as **triangular** (linearly increasing with depth). Three canonical cases appear in KTU problems:

| Case | Condition | Pressure Equation | Shape |
|------|-----------|-------------------|-------|
| 1 | Dry backfill, no surcharge | $p = K_a \cdot \gamma_s \cdot h$ | Triangle starting at 0 at top |
| 2 | Submerged backfill | $p = K_a \cdot \gamma_{sub} \cdot h + \gamma_w \cdot h$ | Triangle + hydrostatic rectangle |
| 3 | Surcharge $q$ on top | $p = K_a \cdot \gamma_s \cdot h + K_a \cdot q$ | Trapezoidal (rectangle + triangle) |

> [!NOTE]
> $K_a = \tan^2(45^\circ - \phi/2)$ is Rankine's **active earth pressure coefficient.**

### 1.6 Creep Line Theory (Bligh's Theory & Khosla's Theory)

A **creep line** is the average path traced by seepage water flowing beneath a hydraulic structure (weir, barrage, head regulator) founded on a pervious foundation. The length of this path is the **hydraulic creep length (L).** It determines the residual uplift pressure at the downstream end.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Bligh's Creep Theory (1910):** The creep length is measured along the **floor** (base of the structure + cut-off depths × safety factor). Uplift at any point is **linearly proportional to the residual creep head.**
>
> **Khosla's Theory (1936):** More accurate; treats the floor as a series of **isolated sheet piles** and uses the **method of independent variables** with charts. It is the **standard method** in KTU and IS:6966.

### 1.7 Uplift Pressure Estimation

**Uplift pressure** is the upward hydrostatic pressure acting on the base of a hydraulic structure due to seepage. It reduces the effective weight and can cause **piping, boiling, or overturning.**

$$\text{Uplift at point } i = \gamma_w \cdot (H - h_i)$$

where $h_i$ is the residual head at point $i$ measured from the downstream water level.

### 1.8 Safety Checks (Limit-State Design)

The four standard stability checks for a head wall / weir / barrage are:

1. **Safety against Sliding:** $FOS_{sliding} = \dfrac{\mu \cdot W + c \cdot A}{P_H} \geq 1.5$
2. **Safety against Overturning:** $FOS_{overturning} = \dfrac{M_R}{M_O} \geq 1.5$ to $2.0$
3. **Safety against Crushing (Max. Compressive Stress):** $\sigma_{max} \leq \sigma_{allowable}$
4. **Safety against Tension (No-Tension Check):** $\sigma_{min} \geq 0$ (no tension at heel)

### 1.9 Chute Spillway

A **chute spillway** is a steep, open-channel spillway consisting of an inlet, a steeply sloping channel (apron/chute), and a terminal energy dissipator. It is used when a dam is too long for an ogee spillway and a separate, independent spillway is needed (e.g., saddle spillway, emergency spillway).

### 1.10 Saint Antony Falls (SAF) Stilling Basin

The **SAF basin** is the standard stilling basin recommended by the **Central Water Commission (CWC), India** for use downstream of weirs, barrages, and small drop structures. It is named after the falls structure in Tamil Nadu where it was first tested. It comprises:

- A horizontal apron.
- A row of **appurtenances** (baffle blocks / chute blocks).
- A dentated (stepped) end sill.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Design Parameter:** The Froude number $Fr_1$ at the basin entrance must lie in the range **2.5 ≤ Fr₁ ≤ 4.5** for stable jump formation in an SAF basin.

### 1.11 Drop Inlet Spillway

A **drop inlet (shaft / morning glory) spillway** is a closed-conduit spillway used when the available spillway crest length is limited. Water enters over a circular (or rectangular) crest, drops vertically through a shaft, and is conveyed through a horizontal or inclined conduit to the downstream channel.

### 1.12 Diversion Structures

A **diversion structure (barrage / weir / head regulator)** diverts water from a natural stream into an off-taking canal. Key elements: **weir wall, under-sluices, divide wall, head regulator, fish ladder, and silt ejector.**

### 1.13 Project Topics (Suggestions)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Project-Phase Suggestions:**
> 1. Design of a SAF stilling basin for a given discharge and head.
> 2. Stability analysis of a head wall for a drop structure (all four checks).
> 3. Khosla's theory-based floor design for a small barrage.
> 4. Hydraulic jump profile computation using Excel/MATLAB.
> 5. Comparative study: Chute vs. Drop Inlet spillway for a saddle site.
> 6. Diversion design for an off-taking canal on a sandy river.

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Hydraulic Jump — Conjugate Depth, Energy Loss, Efficiency

### 2.1.1 Classification of Jump (Based on $Fr_1$)

| $Fr_1$ Range | Jump Type | Characteristics |
|--------------|-----------|-----------------|
| $1.0 - 1.7$ | Undular | Small waves, low energy loss (< 5%) |
| $1.7 - 2.5$ | Weak | Smooth, small rollers, energy loss 5–15% |
| $2.5 - 4.5$ | Oscillating | Unstable, irregular waves, AVOID in design |
| $4.5 - 9.0$ | Steady | Best for energy dissipation |
| $> 9.0$ | Strong / Choppy | Very rough, 70–85% energy loss |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Use:** Engineers design the basin such that the **incoming $Fr_1$ lies between 4.5 and 9.0**, where the jump is steady, well-defined, and energy dissipation is high.

### 2.1.2 Governing Equations

**Momentum Equation** (neglecting bed friction over the short jump length):

$$\frac{Q^2}{g \cdot y_1} + A_1 \cdot \bar{y_1} = \frac{Q^2}{g \cdot y_2} + A_2 \cdot \bar{y_2}$$

For a rectangular channel of unit width ($q = Q/b$):

$$\frac{2 q^2}{g} = y_1 \cdot y_2 \cdot (y_1 + y_2)$$

### 2.1.3 Energy Loss in the Jump

Applying the energy equation between sections 1 and 2:

$$\Delta E = E_1 - E_2 = \frac{(y_2 - y_1)^3}{4 y_1 y_2}$$

The **hydraulic efficiency** of the jump:

$$\eta = \frac{E_2}{E_1} = 1 - \frac{\Delta E}{E_1}$$

### 2.1.4 Length of the Jump

An empirical estimate (USBR):

$$L_j \approx 6.1 \cdot y_2 \quad \text{(for } 4.5 \leq Fr_1 \leq 13.0\text{)}$$

A more conservative estimate (Khurgi):

$$L_j \approx 5.2 \cdot (y_2 - y_1)$$

### 2.1.5 Applications of the Hydraulic Jump

1. **Energy dissipation** downstream of spillways, weirs, and drops.
2. **Raising water levels** on the downstream side (e.g., for irrigation offtake).
3. **Mixing of chemicals** in water treatment (e.g., chlorination).
4. **Aeration** of flows.
5. **Reduction of uplift pressure** on the apron by increasing the residual water depth.

### 2.2 Equivalent Fluid Pressure — Engineering Formulas

| Condition | Active Pressure Intensity at Depth $h$ | Active Force per Unit Length |
|-----------|------------------------------------------|------------------------------|
| Dry backfill, level surface, no surcharge | $p_a = K_a \cdot \gamma_s \cdot h$ | $P_a = \tfrac{1}{2} K_a \gamma_s H^2$ |
| Submerged backfill | $p_a = K_a \cdot \gamma_{sub} \cdot h + \gamma_w h$ | Sum of two triangular forces |
| Surcharge $q$ (kN/m²) on top | $p_a = K_a (q + \gamma_s h)$ | Trapezoidal diagram |
| Cohesive soil (c ≠ 0) | $p_a = K_a \gamma_s h - 2c\sqrt{K_a}$ | Net active force |

**Rankine Coefficients:**

$$K_a = \tan^2\left(45^\circ - \frac{\phi}{2}\right) \quad ; \quad K_p = \tan^2\left(45^\circ + \frac{\phi}{2}\right)$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Equivalent Fluid Unit Weight ($w_e$):** Sometimes textbooks and CWC manuals recommend using an *equivalent fluid unit weight* that already combines $\gamma_s$ and $K_a$:
> $$w_e = K_a \cdot \gamma_s$$
> For saturated submerged backfill, the saturated unit weight is used. The KTU exam frequently uses $w_e = 9.81$ to $11.77$ kN/m³ for typical submerged backfills.

### 2.3 Triangular Load Diagram — Three Cases

**Case A: Active Earth Pressure Only (Dry Backfill)**
- Pressure intensity at depth $h$: $p = K_a \cdot \gamma_s \cdot h$
- Shape: triangle, **zero at top**, maximum at base.
- Total force: $P = \tfrac{1}{2} K_a \gamma_s H^2$
- Acts at $H/3$ from base.

**Case B: Saturated Submerged Backfill**
- Effective stress pressure: $K_a (\gamma_s - \gamma_w) h$
- Pore water pressure: $\gamma_w h$
- Total: $p = K_a \gamma_s h - (K_a - 1) \gamma_w h$
- Shape: triangle with **finite intercept** at the phreatic line.

**Case C: Surcharge + Earth Pressure**
- $p = K_a \cdot q + K_a \gamma_s h$
- Shape: trapezoid — rectangle of intensity $K_a q$ at top + triangle below.

> [!NOTE]
> **Important:** A free-draining **weep holes** or **backfill drain** is provided to ensure that no hydrostatic pressure builds up behind the wall. The KTU design assumes drained backfill unless stated otherwise.

### 2.4 Bligh's Creep Theory (Full Set of Equations)

**Creep Length:**
$$L = b + 2 d_1 + 2 d_2 + \ldots$$
where $b$ is the floor length and $d_i$ are the depths of cut-off walls (each multiplied by a weighting factor).

**Bligh's Creep Coefficient (C):**

| Soil Type | C (Safe) |
|-----------|----------|
| Fine sand | 18 |
| Coarse sand | 15 |
| Gravel | 12 |
| Boulders | 5 to 9 |

**Required Creep Length:**
$$L_{min} = C \cdot H$$
where $H$ = static head (upstream − downstream water level).

**Uplift at distance $x$ from upstream end:**
$$U(x) = \gamma_w \cdot H \cdot \left(1 - \frac{x}{L}\right)$$

This gives a **linear (triangular) uplift pressure diagram** decreasing from $\gamma_w H$ at the upstream end to $0$ at the downstream end.

### 2.5 Khosla's Theory (Modern IS:6966 / IS:7784)

Khosla treated the complex floor profile as a combination of:
1. A **horizontal floor** of finite length.
2. **Vertical sheet piles** at the upstream and downstream ends.

Using the **method of independent variables** (electrical analogy), he developed charts for the **percentage of residual head** at key points on the floor. The key points are:

- **P** = point just downstream of the upstream sheet pile.
- **E** = end point of the floor (just upstream of downstream pile).
- **Intermediate floor points** at 1/4, 1/2, 3/4 of the floor length.

**Khosla's Design Charts** give:
$$\phi_E = \frac{\text{Residual head at E}}{\text{Static head H}}$$

**Corrected Pressures** (after accounting for floor thickness and pile interference):

$$\phi_E^{corrected} = \phi_E \cdot (\text{floor thickness correction}) + (\text{interference correction})$$

**Exit Gradient (Khosla):**

$$G_E = \frac{H}{d} \cdot \frac{1}{\pi \sqrt{\lambda}}$$

where $\lambda = (1 + \sqrt{1 + \alpha^2})/2$ and $\alpha = b/d$.

**Permissible Exit Gradient:**

| Soil | Permissible $G_E$ |
|------|--------------------|
| Fine sand | 1/6 to 1/7 |
| Coarse sand | 1/5 to 1/6 |
| Gravel | 1/4 to 1/5 |

### 2.6 Uplift Pressure Estimation

**Net Effective Weight of Structure:**
$$W_{eff} = W_{structure} - U_{avg}$$

**Net Downward Force** for stability = $W_{eff}$ (effective) − vertical component of any inclined load.

**Khosla Uplift at any point:**

$$U_x = \gamma_w \cdot (H - h_x)$$

where $h_x$ is the residual head at point $x$ read from the **Khosla pressure chart** (after applying corrections).

### 2.7 Stability / Safety Checks — Formulas

**1. Sliding:**
$$FOS = \frac{\mu \sum V + c_b A_b}{\sum H} \geq 1.5$$

where:
- $\sum V$ = sum of vertical forces (weight − uplift)
- $\mu = \tan \phi_b$ = coefficient of base friction (0.45 to 0.7 for concrete on soil)
- $c_b$ = base cohesion
- $A_b$ = base area
- $\sum H$ = sum of horizontal forces (water pressure, earth pressure, surcharge)

**2. Overturning:**
$$FOS = \frac{M_R}{M_O} = \frac{\sum (\text{Resisting Moments})}{\sum (\text{Overturning Moments})} \geq 1.5$$

**3. Crushing (Max. Compressive Stress at Toe):**

$$\sigma_{max} = \frac{\sum V}{b} \left(1 + \frac{6 e}{b}\right) \leq \sigma_{c,allowable}$$

where $e$ is the **eccentricity** of the resultant from the center of the base.

**4. Tension (No-Tension Check at Heel):**

$$\sigma_{min} = \frac{\sum V}{b} \left(1 - \frac{6 e}{b}\right) \geq 0$$

(no tension ⇒ $e \leq b/6$ → **middle-third rule**)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Middle-Third Rule:** For a gravity dam, weir, or head wall, the **resultant of all forces** must intersect the base within the **middle third** of the base width. If $e > b/6$, tension develops at the heel ⇒ unsafe.

### 2.8 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula | Notes |
|---|---------|---------|-------|
| 1 | Froude Number | $Fr = V/\sqrt{gy}$ | Unit: dimensionless |
| 2 | Bélanger (conjugate depth) | $y_2/y_1 = \tfrac{1}{2}(\sqrt{1+8Fr_1^2}-1)$ | Rectangular channel |
| 3 | Energy loss in jump | $\Delta E = (y_2-y_1)^3 / (4y_1 y_2)$ | Head loss in metres |
| 4 | Jump length (USBR) | $L_j = 6.1 y_2$ | For steady jump |
| 5 | Rankine $K_a$ | $K_a = \tan^2(45^\circ - \phi/2)$ | Active |
| 6 | Active force | $P_a = \tfrac{1}{2} K_a \gamma_s H^2$ | Dry, level, no surcharge |
| 7 | EFP unit weight | $w_e = K_a \gamma_s$ | Equivalent fluid (submerged) |
| 8 | Bligh creep length | $L = C \cdot H$ | C depends on soil |
| 9 | Bligh uplift | $U = \gamma_w H (1 - x/L)$ | Linear, triangular |

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Bélanger Equation (Conjugate Depth Ratio)

**Step 1 — Setup:** Consider a hydraulic jump in a **horizontal, rectangular, prismatic channel** of width $b$. The jump occurs between section 1 (just before, supercritical) and section 2 (just after, subcritical).

Let:
- $y_1, V_1$ = depth and velocity at section 1.
- $y_2, V_2$ = depth and velocity at section 2.
- $q = Q/b$ = discharge per unit width (constant).

**Step 2 — Continuity Equation:**

$$q = V_1 y_1 = V_2 y_2 \quad \Rightarrow \quad V_1 = \frac{q}{y_1}, \quad V_2 = \frac{q}{y_2}$$

**Step 3 — Momentum Equation (per unit width):**
The net hydrostatic force on a control volume equals the rate of change of momentum.

$$\rho q (V_2 - V_1) = \frac{1}{2}\rho g (y_1^2 - y_2^2)$$

Dividing both sides by $\rho g$:

$$\frac{q}{g}(V_2 - V_1) = \frac{1}{2}(y_1^2 - y_2^2)$$

**Step 4 — Substitute $V_1, V_2$:**

$$\frac{q}{g}\left(\frac{q}{y_2} - \frac{q}{y_1}\right) = \frac{1}{2}(y_1^2 - y_2^2)$$

$$\frac{q^2}{g}\left(\frac{y_1 - y_2}{y_1 y_2}\right) = \frac{1}{2}(y_1 - y_2)(y_1 + y_2)$$

**Step 5 — Cancel $(y_1 - y_2)$** (non-trivial, since $y_1 \ne y_2$):

$$\frac{2q^2}{g y_1 y_2} = y_1 + y_2$$

**Step 6 — Introduce $Fr_1$:** Recall $Fr_1^2 = V_1^2/(g y_1) = q^2/(g y_1^3)$. So $q^2 = Fr_1^2 g y_1^3$.

$$\frac{2 Fr_1^2 g y_1^3}{g y_1 y_2} = y_1 + y_2 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \frac{2 Fr_1^2 y_1^2}{y_2} = y_1 + y_2$$

Multiplying by $y_2$ and rearranging:

$$2 Fr_1^2 y_1^2 = y_2^2 + y_1 y_2$$

This is a quadratic in $y_2$:

$$y_2^2 + y_1 y_2 - 2 Fr_1^2 y_1^2 = 0$$

**Step 7 — Solve for $y_2$:** Using the quadratic formula $y_2 = \dfrac{-y_1 + \sqrt{y_1^2 + 8 Fr_1^2 y_1^2}}{2}$ (taking the positive root):

$$\boxed{\;\frac{y_2}{y_1} = \frac{1}{2}\left(\sqrt{1 + 8 Fr_1^2} - 1\right)\;}$$

This is the **Bélanger Equation** — the cornerstone of hydraulic jump analysis. $\blacksquare$

---

## 3.2 Derivation of the Energy Loss in a Hydraulic Jump

**Step 1 — Energy at section 1 (per unit weight):**

$$E_1 = y_1 + \frac{V_1^2}{2g} = y_1 + \frac{q^2}{2g y_1^2}$$

**Step 2 — Energy at section 2:**

$$E_2 = y_2 + \frac{q^2}{2g y_2^2}$$

**Step 3 — Energy loss:**

$$\Delta E = E_1 - E_2 = (y_1 - y_2) + \frac{q^2}{2g}\left(\frac{1}{y_1^2} - \frac{1}{y_2^2}\right)$$

Factorising:

$$\Delta E = (y_1 - y_2) + \frac{q^2}{2g} \cdot \frac{(y_2^2 - y_1^2)}{y_1^2 y_2^2} = (y_1 - y_2) + \frac{q^2(y_1+y_2)(y_2-y_1)(y_2+y_1)}{2g \cdot y_1^2 y_2^2}$$

Wait — let us factor more cleanly. The key identity from Step 5 of §3.1 is:

$$\frac{q^2}{g} = \frac{y_1 y_2 (y_1 + y_2)}{2}$$

So:

$$\frac{q^2}{2g} = \frac{y_1 y_2 (y_1 + y_2)}{4}$$

Substituting:

$$\Delta E = (y_1 - y_2) + \frac{y_1 y_2 (y_1 + y_2)}{4} \cdot \left(\frac{y_2 - y_1}{y_1 y_2}\right) \cdot \frac{1}{y_1 y_2}$$

A cleaner approach — write:

$$\frac{1}{y_1^2} - \frac{1}{y_2^2} = \frac{y_2^2 - y_1^2}{y_1^2 y_2^2} = \frac{(y_2 - y_1)(y_2 + y_1)}{y_1^2 y_2^2}$$

Then:

$$\Delta E = (y_1 - y_2) + \frac{y_1 y_2 (y_1 + y_2)}{4} \cdot \frac{(y_2 - y_1)(y_2 + y_1)}{y_1^2 y_2^2}$$

$$= (y_1 - y_2)\left[1 - \frac{(y_1 + y_2)^2}{4 y_1 y_2}\right] = (y_1 - y_2) \cdot \frac{4 y_1 y_2 - (y_1 + y_2)^2}{4 y_1 y_2}$$

The numerator: $4y_1 y_2 - (y_1 + y_2)^2 = -y_1^2 - y_2^2 + 2 y_1 y_2 = -(y_1 - y_2)^2$.

Therefore:

$$\Delta E = (y_1 - y_2) \cdot \frac{-(y_1 - y_2)^2}{4 y_1 y_2} = \frac{(y_2 - y_1)^3}{4 y_1 y_2}$$

$$\boxed{\;\Delta E = \frac{(y_2 - y_1)^3}{4 y_1 y_2}\;}$$

**Engineering insight:** The energy loss is proportional to the **cube of the rise in depth** — a small rise dissipates very little; a strong jump (large $y_2$) dissipates most of the kinetic energy. $\blacksquare$

---

## 3.3 Derivation of the Exit Gradient (Khosla's Theory)

**Step 1 — Define exit gradient** as the rate of head loss per unit length at the downstream end of the floor, just at the point of emergence of the seepage line on the downstream side.

**Step 2 — Schaffernak's & Khosla's formula (for a floor of length $b$ with downstream pile of depth $d$):**

$$G_E = \frac{H}{d} \cdot \frac{1}{\pi \sqrt{\lambda}}$$

where $\lambda = \dfrac{1 + \sqrt{1 + \alpha^2}}{2}$ and $\alpha = b/d$.

**Step 3 — Derivation of $\lambda$ (outline):** Khosla's analysis treats the floor and sheet pile as a potential flow problem (Laplace equation). The solution is identical in form to the **electrical resistance problem of a strip with a deep notch.** The factor $\lambda$ emerges as the eigenvalue of the conformal map $\zeta = \sqrt{z^2 + d^2}$ applied to the boundary conditions.

A key intermediate identity used in IS:7784 charts:

$$\phi_E = \frac{1}{\pi} \cos^{-1}\left(\frac{\alpha - 2}{\alpha + 2}\right)$$

This gives the **residual head at the end of the floor E** as a fraction of the total head H. From this, the exit gradient follows by differentiation.

**Step 4 — Numerical form:** If $\alpha = b/d = 4$, then $\sqrt{1 + \alpha^2} = \sqrt{17} \approx 4.123$, $\lambda = (1 + 4.123)/2 = 2.56$, $\sqrt{\lambda} = 1.6$, and $G_E = H/(d \pi \cdot 1.6) = 0.199 \cdot H/d$.

For sand with permissible $G_E = 1/6$, the required $d \geq 1.2 H$. This single line explains the **deep downstream cut-off** seen in Indian barrages.

---

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example — Hydraulic Jump Stability of Head Wall

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem (Typical KTU 14-Mark Question):** A hydraulic jump has $V_1 = 7$ m/s, $y_1 = 0.4$ m in a rectangular channel. Find (a) the sequent depth, (b) the energy loss, (c) the efficiency, and (d) the jump length.

**Step (a) — Sequent depth:**

$$Fr_1 = \frac{V_1}{\sqrt{g y_1}} = \frac{7}{\sqrt{9.81 \times 0.4}} = \frac{7}{1.981} = 3.534$$

$$\frac{y_2}{y_1} = \frac{1}{2}\left(\sqrt{1 + 8(3.534)^2} - 1\right) = \frac{1}{2}\left(\sqrt{1 + 99.92} - 1\right)$$

$$= \frac{1}{2}\left(\sqrt{100.92} - 1\right) = \frac{1}{2}(10.046 - 1) = 4.523$$

$$y_2 = 4.523 \times 0.4 = 1.809 \text{ m}$$

**Step (b) — Energy loss:**

$$\Delta E = \frac{(1.809 - 0.4)^3}{4 \times 0.4 \times 1.809} = \frac{(1.409)^3}{2.894} = \frac{2.797}{2.894} = 0.967 \text{ m}$$

**Step (c) — Efficiency:**

$$E_1 = y_1 + \frac{V_1^2}{2g} = 0.4 + \frac{49}{19.62} = 0.4 + 2.497 = 2.897 \text{ m}$$

$$\eta = \frac{E_2}{E_1} = \frac{E_1 - \Delta E}{E_1} = \frac{2.897 - 0.967}{2.897} = 0.666 \text{ or } 66.6\%$$

**Step (d) — Jump length (USBR):**

$$L_j = 6.1 y_2 = 6.1 \times 1.809 = 11.03 \text{ m}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Sanity check:** Since $Fr_1 = 3.534$, the jump is in the **oscillating range (2.5–4.5)**. The KTU examiner will deduct marks if the student does not comment on this and suggest redesigning for $Fr_1 \in [4.5, 9.0]$.

---

## 3.5 Worked Example — Head Wall Stability (All Four Checks)

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem:** A vertical head wall retains water 3 m deep on the upstream side, has a base width 3.0 m, vertical back face, $\gamma_{concrete} = 24$ kN/m³, $\mu = 0.6$, $K_a = 0.33$, $\phi = 30°$, $\gamma_s = 18$ kN/m³. Check for sliding, overturning, crushing, and tension. Take $\sigma_{allowable} = 4500$ kN/m².

**Step 1 — Forces (per unit length of wall, 1 m strip):**
- Vertical water pressure on upstream face = hydrostatic = $\tfrac{1}{2}\gamma_w h^2 = 0.5 \times 9.81 \times 9 = 44.15$ kN → horizontal.
- Active earth pressure (backfill, dry): $P_a = \tfrac{1}{2} K_a \gamma_s H^2 = 0.5 \times 0.33 \times 18 \times 9 = 26.73$ kN → horizontal.
- Total horizontal force $H = 44.15 + 26.73 = 70.88$ kN.
- Self-weight of wall: $W = 24 \times 3 \times 3 = 216$ kN (assuming rectangular, but here we assume unit length).
- Uplift (assume linear, full base, max = $\gamma_w h = 9.81 \times 3 = 29.43$ kN/m²; average = 14.715 kN/m²).
- Total uplift $U = 14.715 \times 3 = 44.15$ kN.

**Step 2 — Sliding:**

$$FOS = \frac{\mu (W - U)}{H} = \frac{0.6 \times (216 - 44.15)}{70.88} = \frac{0.6 \times 171.85}{70.88} = \frac{103.11}{70.88} = 1.455$$

❌ **FAILS** (required ≥ 1.5). Wall is unsafe against sliding.

**Step 3 — Overturning:**
Take moments about the toe.
- Resisting moment: $W \times (b/2) = 216 \times 1.5 = 324$ kN·m
- Resisting moment from uplift: subtract uplift moment about toe. Uplift triangular from $0$ at toe to $\gamma_w H$ at heel. Moment of uplift about toe = $\frac{1}{2}\gamma_w H \cdot b \cdot \frac{b}{3} = 0.5 \times 29.43 \times 3 \times 1 = 44.15$ kN·m.
- Net resisting: $324 - 44.15 = 279.85$ kN·m.
- Overturning: water force at $h/3 = 1$ m: $44.15 \times 1 = 44.15$ kN·m; earth force at $H/3 = 1$ m: $26.73 \times 1 = 26.73$ kN·m. Total = $70.88$ kN·m.

$$FOS = \frac{279.85}{70.88} = 3.95 \;\; ✓ \text{ (Safe)}$$

**Step 4 — Eccentricity & Stress:**

$$\bar{x} = \frac{M_R - M_O}{W_{eff}} = \frac{279.85 - 70.88}{171.85} = \frac{208.97}{171.85} = 1.216 \text{ m from toe}$$

$$e = \frac{b}{2} - \bar{x} = 1.5 - 1.216 = 0.284 \text{ m}$$

Check: $b/6 = 0.5$ m. Since $e = 0.284 < 0.5$ m → ✓ **no tension at heel**.

**Step 5 — Max and Min Stress (Crushing + Tension):**

$$\sigma_{max} = \frac{W_{eff}}{b}\left(1 + \frac{6e}{b}\right) = \frac{171.85}{3}\left(1 + \frac{6 \times 0.284}{3}\right) = 57.28 \times (1 + 0.568) = 89.82 \text{ kN/m}^2$$

$$\sigma_{min} = \frac{W_{eff}}{b}\left(1 - \frac{6e}{b}\right) = 57.28 \times (1 - 0.568) = 24.74 \text{ kN/m}^2 \; > 0 \;\; ✓$$

Both stresses ≪ $\sigma_{allowable} = 4500$ kN/m² → ✓ **Safe against crushing and tension**.

**Conclusion:** Wall passes three checks but **fails sliding**. The KTU examiner expects a recommendation: increase base width, add a shear key, or increase $\mu$ (e.g., rough base) to achieve $FOS_{sliding} \geq 1.5$.

## 3.6 KTU Analytical Matrix — Stilling Basin Selection

| $Fr_1$ | Jump Type | Recommended Basin |
|---------|-----------|-------------------|
| < 1.7 | Undular | No basin needed |
| 1.7 – 2.5 | Weak | Plain apron, dentated end sill |
| 2.5 – 4.5 | Oscillating | **AVOID** — provide chute blocks or redesign |
| 4.5 – 9.0 | Steady | **SAF basin** (best, optimal) |
| > 9.0 | Strong | USBR Type IV basin (impact + baffle) |

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — 3-Mark Short-Answer Questions

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Define the hydraulic jump and state its four practical applications in hydraulic engineering.**

**Model Answer:**
A hydraulic jump is a rapidly varied, turbulent transition from supercritical to subcritical flow, accompanied by a sudden rise in water surface, air entrainment, and a large head loss.

**Applications:**
1. **Energy dissipation** downstream of weirs, barrages, spillways, and drops.
2. **Raising upstream water levels** to facilitate diversion into canals.
3. **Mixing of chemicals** in water treatment plants.
4. **Aeration** of flows in rivers and treatment plants.

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - June 2024]`
**What is meant by 'equivalent fluid pressure' on a head wall? List any four variables that affect it.**

**Model Answer:**
Equivalent fluid pressure (EFP) is a design simplification in which lateral earth pressure is replaced by hydrostatic pressure of a fictitious fluid of unit weight $w_e = K_a \gamma_s$. This makes computations convenient.

**Variables affecting EFP:**
1. **Angle of internal friction $\phi$** of the backfill soil.
2. **Unit weight of soil** (dry, moist, or saturated).
3. **Surcharge load** (if any) on top of the backfill.
4. **Drainage condition** (presence or absence of weep holes).

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Per KTU 2024 ESE pattern, each Part-B question carries 14 marks. Internal choice is mandatory. The two questions below are independent alternatives.**

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Adapted]`

**(a)** A hydraulic jump occurs in a horizontal, rectangular channel 4 m wide, with $V_1 = 8$ m/s and $y_1 = 0.5$ m. Determine the **sequent depth $y_2$**, the **energy loss $\Delta E$**, the **specific energy at section 2**, and the **length of the jump**. Comment on the type of jump. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the **Bélanger equation** and derive it from first principles using the **continuity and momentum equations**. State the assumptions made. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Question A (a)

**Step 1 — Froude Number at Section 1:**

$$Fr_1 = \frac{V_1}{\sqrt{g y_1}} = \frac{8}{\sqrt{9.81 \times 0.5}} = \frac{8}{2.215} = 3.611$$

**Step 2 — Sequent Depth Ratio:**

$$\frac{y_2}{y_1} = \frac{1}{2}\left(\sqrt{1 + 8 Fr_1^2} - 1\right) = \frac{1}{2}\left(\sqrt{1 + 8(13.04)} - 1\right)$$

$$= \frac{1}{2}\left(\sqrt{105.32} - 1\right) = \frac{1}{2}(10.262 - 1) = 4.631$$

$$y_2 = 4.631 \times 0.5 = 2.316 \text{ m}$$

**Step 3 — Energy at Section 1:**

$$E_1 = y_1 + \frac{V_1^2}{2g} = 0.5 + \frac{64}{19.62} = 0.5 + 3.262 = 3.762 \text{ m}$$

**Step 4 — Energy Loss:**

$$\Delta E = \frac{(y_2 - y_1)^3}{4 y_1 y_2} = \frac{(2.316 - 0.5)^3}{4 \times 0.5 \times 2.316} = \frac{(1.816)^3}{4.632} = \frac{5.992}{4.632} = 1.294 \text{ m}$$

**Step 5 — Energy at Section 2:**

$$E_2 = E_1 - \Delta E = 3.762 - 1.294 = 2.468 \text{ m}$$

**Step 6 — Jump Length (USBR):**

$$L_j = 6.1 y_2 = 6.1 \times 2.316 = 14.13 \text{ m}$$

**Step 7 — Comment on jump type:** Since $Fr_1 = 3.611$ which lies between 2.5 and 4.5, the jump is **oscillating** — unstable with pulsating waves. **Recommendation:** Redesign by adding chute blocks at the entrance or modify the upstream slope to achieve $Fr_1 \in [4.5, 9.0]$ for a stable jump suitable for a SAF stilling basin.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall:** Students often write $L_j = 5(y_2 - y_1) = 5 \times 1.816 = 9.08$ m using the older USBR formula. While not strictly wrong, the **6.1 y₂ formula is preferred for steady jumps** and is the current IS:4997 recommendation. Also: students forget to **identify the jump type** — losing 1 mark.

---

#### Model Solution for Question A (b)

**Derivation of the Bélanger Equation — see Section 3.1 above for the full derivation.** The student must include:

1. **Assumptions:** horizontal, rectangular, prismatic, frictionless, hydrostatic pressure distribution.
2. **Continuity:** $V_1 y_1 = V_2 y_2 = q$.
3. **Momentum equation** written in standard form, with substitution of $V_1, V_2$.
4. **Algebraic manipulation** leading to the quadratic in $y_2$ and the final result.
5. **Discussion** of when the equation is valid (any $Fr_1 > 1$).

A sample concise final form:

$$\frac{y_2}{y_1} = \frac{1}{2}\left(\sqrt{1 + 8 Fr_1^2} - 1\right)$$

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Adapted]`

**(a)** A vertical **head wall** of a drop structure retains water 3.5 m deep on the upstream face, and dry cohesionless backfill of $\phi = 30°$ and $\gamma_s = 18$ kN/m³ on the downstream face. The wall is **rectangular**, 3.5 m wide at the base, 4.5 m tall, with $\gamma_{concrete} = 24$ kN/m³ and base friction coefficient $\mu = 0.6$. The **uplift** varies linearly from $\gamma_w h$ at the heel to $0$ at the toe. Check the wall for:
- (i) **Sliding** (FOS ≥ 1.5)
- (ii) **Overturning** (FOS ≥ 1.5)
- (iii) **Tension at heel** (no tension ⇒ e ≤ b/6)
- (iv) **Crushing at toe** (σ_max ≤ 4500 kN/m²) **(10 marks)**

**(b)** Sketch the **triangular load diagram** for a head wall with (i) dry backfill, and (ii) submerged backfill, indicating the magnitudes of the pressure intensities. List the **variables affecting equivalent fluid pressure**. **(4 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Question B (a)

**Step 1 — Rankine active coefficient:**

$$K_a = \tan^2(45 - \phi/2) = \tan^2(30°) = 0.333$$

**Step 2 — Active earth pressure force (dry backfill, no surcharge):**

$$P_a = \frac{1}{2} K_a \gamma_s H^2 = \frac{1}{2}(0.333)(18)(3.5)^2 = \frac{1}{2}(0.333)(18)(12.25) = 36.71 \text{ kN/m}$$

**Step 3 — Hydrostatic force from upstream water (3.5 m deep):**

$$P_w = \frac{1}{2}\gamma_w H_w^2 = \frac{1}{2}(9.81)(3.5)^2 = \frac{1}{2}(9.81)(12.25) = 60.09 \text{ kN/m}$$

**Step 4 — Total horizontal force:**

$$\sum H = 60.09 + 36.71 = 96.80 \text{ kN/m}$$

**Step 5 — Self-weight of wall (rectangular, 3.5 m base × 4.5 m height):**

$$W = \gamma_c \cdot b \cdot h = 24 \times 3.5 \times 4.5 = 378 \text{ kN/m}$$

**Step 6 — Uplift (linear, full base):**

$$U_{avg} = \frac{1}{2}\gamma_w H = \frac{1}{2}(9.81)(3.5) = 17.17 \text{ kN/m}^2$$

$$U_{total} = U_{avg} \times b = 17.17 \times 3.5 = 60.10 \text{ kN/m}$$

**Step 7 — Net vertical force:**

$$W_{eff} = W - U = 378 - 60.10 = 317.90 \text{ kN/m}$$

---

**(i) Sliding check:**

$$FOS_{sliding} = \frac{\mu \cdot W_{eff}}{\sum H} = \frac{0.6 \times 317.90}{96.80} = \frac{190.74}{96.80} = 1.971$$

✅ **SAFE** ($FOS \geq 1.5$).

---

**(ii) Overturning check (moments about toe):**

- Resisting moment from weight: $W \times (b/2) = 378 \times 1.75 = 661.5$ kN·m/m.
- Overturning moment from uplift (rectangle, no moment about toe if uniform; here triangular so it produces moment). For linear uplift with max at heel and 0 at toe: moment of uplift about toe = $\tfrac{1}{3} \gamma_w H \cdot b \cdot b = \tfrac{1}{3}(9.81)(3.5)(3.5)(3.5) = 140.23$ kN·m.

Wait — for a linear (triangular) uplift with 0 at toe and $\gamma_w H$ at heel, the centroid is at $2b/3$ from toe.

$$M_{uplift} = U_{total} \times \frac{2b}{3} = 60.10 \times \frac{2 \times 3.5}{3} = 140.23 \text{ kN·m/m}$$

- Net resisting moment: $M_R = 661.5 - 140.23 = 521.27$ kN·m/m.
- Overturning moment from water (at $h/3$ from base): $P_w \times h/3 = 60.09 \times 1.167 = 70.11$ kN·m/m.
- Overturning moment from earth pressure (at $H/3$ from base): $P_a \times H/3 = 36.71 \times 1.167 = 42.83$ kN·m/m.
- Total overturning: $M_O = 70.11 + 42.83 = 112.94$ kN·m/m.

$$FOS_{overturning} = \frac{M_R}{M_O} = \frac{521.27}{112.94} = 4.617$$

✅ **SAFE** ($FOS \geq 1.5$).

---

**(iii) Tension check (middle-third rule):**

$$\bar{x} = \frac{M_R - M_O}{W_{eff}} = \frac{521.27 - 112.94}{317.90} = \frac{408.33}{317.90} = 1.285 \text{ m from toe}$$

$$e = \frac{b}{2} - \bar{x} = 1.75 - 1.285 = 0.465 \text{ m}$$

Check: $b/6 = 3.5/6 = 0.583$ m. Since $e = 0.465 < 0.583$ → ✅ **No tension at heel**.

---

**(iv) Crushing check (max stress at toe):**

$$\sigma_{max} = \frac{W_{eff}}{b}\left(1 + \frac{6e}{b}\right) = \frac{317.90}{3.5}\left(1 + \frac{6 \times 0.465}{3.5}\right) = 90.83 \times (1 + 0.797) = 90.83 \times 1.797$$

$$\sigma_{max} = 163.21 \text{ kN/m}^2 \ll 4500 \text{ kN/m}^2 \;\; ✅ \text{ SAFE}$$

$$\sigma_{min} = 90.83 \times (1 - 0.797) = 90.83 \times 0.203 = 18.43 \text{ kN/m}^2 > 0 \;\; ✅$$

---

**Overall Conclusion:** The head wall is **safe** against all four modes of failure.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall — Frequent Mistakes:**
> 1. **Sign convention error:** Treating uplift as a *vertical downward* force (it's a vertical upward force, reducing effective weight).
> 2. **Forgetting that the resultant of triangular uplift passes at 2b/3 from the toe** (or b/3 from the heel). This is where 2 marks are routinely lost.
> 3. **Confusing unit weight of water** — 9.81 kN/m³ is the standard, but 1000 kgf/m³ must NOT be used unless explicitly asked.
> 4. **Forgetting to include the active earth pressure** on the downstream (backfill) side — this is the most common single error in KTU papers, costing 2–3 marks per question.

---

#### Model Solution for Question B (b)

**Triangular load diagram (dry backfill):**

> Pressure intensity at depth $h$: $p = K_a \cdot \gamma_s \cdot h$ — a **triangle** starting at **0 at the top** of the wall and growing to $p_{max} = K_a \gamma_s H$ at the **base**. Total force = $\tfrac{1}{2} K_a \gamma_s H^2$, acting at $H/3$ from the base.

**Triangular load diagram (submerged backfill):**

> Pressure intensity at depth $h$ below the phreatic line: $p = K_a \gamma_{sub} h + \gamma_w h$. This is a **composite triangle** (effective stress triangle + hydrostatic triangle), or treated as **two separate triangles** for force computation: $P_1 = \tfrac{1}{2} K_a \gamma_{sub} H^2$ and $P_2 = \tfrac{1}{2} \gamma_w H^2$.

**Variables affecting EFP (4 of 7):**
1. Angle of internal friction ($\phi$)
2. Unit weight of soil ($\gamma_s$)
3. Surcharge load ($q$)
4. Drainage / saturation condition (whether backfill is dry or submerged)
5. (Optional) Backfill slope angle ($\beta$)
6. (Optional) Wall friction angle ($\delta$)
7. (Optional) Wall movement type (active / at-rest / passive)

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Module 4 — One-Page Revision**

### Hydraulic Jump
- [ ] Definition: supercritical → subcritical transition with energy dissipation.
- [ ] **Bélanger equation:** $y_2/y_1 = \tfrac{1}{2}(\sqrt{1 + 8Fr_1^2} - 1)$.
- [ ] **Energy loss:** $\Delta E = (y_2 - y_1)^3 / (4 y_1 y_2)$.
- [ ] **Jump length (USBR):** $L_j = 6.1 y_2$.
- [ ] **Optimal jump range:** $4.5 \leq Fr_1 \leq 9.0$ → steady, well-defined.
- [ ] **AVOID** $Fr_1 \in [2.5, 4.5]$ → oscillating, unstable.
- [ ] **Applications:** energy dissipation, water-level raising, mixing, aeration.

### Equivalent Fluid Pressure (EFP)
- [ ] $p = w_e h$, where $w_e = K_a \gamma_s$.
- [ ] **Triangular diagrams** (dry, submerged, surcharge) — see Section 2.3 table.
- [ ] **Rankine $K_a = \tan^2(45° - \phi/2)$** for level backfill.
- [ ] **Total force** = area of pressure diagram.
- [ ] **Point of application** = centroid of diagram (H/3 from base for triangle).

### Creep Line Theory
- [ ] **Bligh:** $L = C \cdot H$, with $C$ tabulated by soil type.
- [ ] **Khosla:** charts for residual head; exit gradient $G_E = (H/d)/(\pi\sqrt{\lambda})$.
- [ ] **Permissible $G_E$:** 1/6 (fine sand), 1/5 (coarse), 1/4 (gravel).

### Uplift Pressure
- [ ] **Bligh:** linear, $U = \gamma_w H (1 - x/L)$.
- [ ] **Khosla:** non-linear, read from charts with corrections.
- [ ] Reduces effective weight ⇒ reduces sliding and overturning safety.

### Safety Checks
- [ ] **Sliding:** $FOS = (\mu W_{eff})/H \geq 1.5$.
- [ ] **Overturning:** $FOS = M_R/M_O \geq 1.5$.
- [ ] **Tension:** $e \leq b/6$ (middle-third rule).
- [ ] **Crushing:** $\sigma_{max} = (W_{eff}/b)(1 + 6e/b) \leq \sigma_{allow}$.

### Chute Spillway
- [ ] Steep open-channel spillway, used when ogee is uneconomical.
- [ ] **Components:** inlet, control section, chute, transition, energy dissipator.
- [ ] **Energy dissipators:** SAF basin (Fr 2.5–4.5), USBR Type IV (Fr > 4.5).

### SAF Stilling Basin
- [ ] **CWC-recommended** basin for Indian weirs/barrages.
- [ ] **Components:** chute blocks, plain apron, dentated end sill.
- [ ] **Design range:** $Fr_1 \in [2.5, 4.5]$.
- [ ] **Basin length** $L_b \approx 4$ to $5 D_2$.
- [ ] **End sill height** $\approx 0.15 D_2$.
- [ ] **Limitations:** not for $Fr_1 > 4.5$ (use USBR); scour still possible downstream.

### Drop Inlet Spillway
- [ ] Used when **crest length is limited** (e.g., saddle dam).
- [ ] Components: **crest, vertical shaft, conduit, stilling basin**.
- [ ] Provides **anti-vortex plate** and **trash rack**.
- [ ] **Design based on orifice + weir flow** combination.

### Diversion Structures
- [ ] Components: **weir, under-sluices, divide wall, head regulator, fish ladder**.
- [ ] Khosla's theory used for floor design.
- [ ] Silt ejector essential for canal longevity.

### Project Topics
- [ ] SAF basin design; head wall stability; Khosla floor design; jump profiles.
